changes and running automated tests to catch integration problems early,
which is a technical practice that doesn\'t directly create shared
vision with customers.
erro_alternative3:
Pair programming involves two developers working together at one
workstation, improving code quality and knowledge sharing within the
development team but not specifically creating shared vision with
customers.
erro_alternative4:
Test-driven development writes tests before code to drive design and
ensure quality, which is a technical development practice that doesn\'t
directly involve customers or create shared understanding of user needs.

statement:
A project manager needs to communicate a complex change to project
requirements that will affect multiple teams across different time
zones. The communication must be precise, provide detailed
specifications, serve as a reference document, and create an auditable
record of what was approved. What type of communication should the project manager use?
option1: Informal verbal communication
option2: Interactive communication
option3: Written communication
option4: Nonverbal communication
explanation:
Written communication includes emails, reports, memos, letters, and
formal documents that create permanent records of information. Written
communication is most appropriate when precision and detail are
critical, when information needs to be referenced repeatedly, when
creating auditable records for compliance or legal purposes, when
communicating across time zones where synchronous communication is
difficult, and when the same detailed message must reach multiple
recipients consistently. For complex changes affecting multiple teams,
written communication ensures everyone receives identical information,
can review details at their own pace, and has documentation to reference
during implementation.
erro_alternative1:
Informal verbal communication is spontaneous and unstructured, lacking
the precision, detail, and documentation needed for communicating
complex changes that require permanent records.
erro_alternative2:
Interactive communication involves real-time dialogue like meetings or
phone calls, which doesn\'t create the detailed documented record needed
across time zones or provide reference material for later use.
erro_alternative4:
Nonverbal communication consists of body language, gestures, and facial
expressions that supplement verbal messages but cannot convey the
complex detailed specifications needed for requirement changes.

statement:
A project manager needs to immediately notify all team members and key
stakeholders about a critical schedule change that affects work starting
next week. The project manager wants to ensure everyone receives this
time-sensitive information directly without waiting for them to check
the project portal. What communication method should the project manager use?
option1: Pull communication
option2: Push communication
option3: Interactive communication
option4: Informal communication
explanation:
Push communication sends information directly to specific recipients
whether they requested it or not, ensuring they receive critical or
time-sensitive information. Examples include emails, memos, letters,
reports, faxes, voicemail messages, and direct notifications sent to
defined distribution lists. Push communication is most appropriate when
information is urgent, when the sender needs to ensure specific
recipients receive the information, when recipients might not know to
look for the information proactively, and when timely delivery is
critical. The sender controls timing and distribution, making it ideal
for announcements, critical updates, and mandatory communications.
erro_alternative1:
Pull communication makes information available in a central location for
recipients to access when they choose, which doesn\'t ensure timely
receipt of urgent information since people may not check frequently
enough.
erro_alternative3:
Interactive communication involves real-time, multidirectional exchange
through meetings or calls, which is less efficient than push methods for
simply notifying multiple recipients of a schedule change.
erro_alternative4:
Informal communication describes the casual, unstructured nature of
exchanges but doesn\'t specify the delivery method of directly sending
information to ensure recipients receive it.

statement:
A project manager is working through the execution phase and is focused
on creating project status updates, distributing team meeting minutes,
updating the shared project repository with current documentation, and
sending milestone notifications to stakeholders as defined in the
communications management plan. What process is the project manager performing?
option1: Plan Communications Management
option2: Manage Communications
option3: Control Communications
option4: Monitor Stakeholder Engagement
explanation:
Manage Communications is the executing process of creating, collecting,
distributing, storing, retrieving, and managing project information
according to the communications management plan. This process involves
producing actual communications (status reports, updates,
presentations), distributing information through defined channels to
appropriate recipients, storing information in accessible repositories,
and ensuring stakeholders receive the information they need. Manage
Communications implements the strategies and approaches defined during
planning, turning the communications management plan into actual
information delivery throughout project execution.
erro_alternative1:
Plan Communications Management occurs during planning to assess
stakeholder information needs and develop the approach for project
communications, not the actual execution of creating and distributing
information.
erro_alternative3:
Control Communications is not a standard PMBOK process name. The
monitoring process is \"Monitor Communications\" which assesses
communication effectiveness rather than executing information creation
and distribution.
erro_alternative4:
Monitor Stakeholder Engagement tracks overall stakeholder relationships
and engagement levels, which is broader than the specific activities of
creating and distributing project information.

statement:
A project manager is working with a software development team that
follows a practice where developers write automated tests that initially
fail, then write the minimum code necessary to make those tests pass,
and finally refactor the code to improve its design while keeping tests
passing. What development practice is the team using?
option1: Continuous integration
option2: Pair programming
option3: Code reviews
option4: Test-driven development
explanation:
Test-driven development (TDD) is a software development practice where
developers write automated tests before writing the code that makes
those tests pass. The TDD cycle follows \"Red-Green-Refactor\": write a
test that fails (red), write minimal code to make it pass (green), then
refactor to improve design while maintaining passing tests. TDD is a
core practice in Extreme Programming (XP) and ensures code is testable
by design, provides immediate feedback on functionality, creates a
comprehensive test suite automatically, and drives simpler design by
focusing on making tests pass rather than building elaborate frameworks
upfront.
erro_alternative1:
Continuous integration is the practice of frequently merging code
changes and running automated builds and tests to catch integration
problems early, but doesn\'t specifically describe writing tests before
code.
erro_alternative2:
Pair programming involves two developers working together at one
workstation, with one writing code and the other reviewing in real-time,
but doesn\'t specify the test-first approach.
erro_alternative3:
Code reviews are structured examinations of code by other developers to
identify defects and improvement opportunities, conducted after code is
written rather than driving the coding process with tests.

statement:
A project manager needs to create a document that will track all
identified risks throughout the project, including their descriptions,
categories, causes, probability and impact ratings, priority rankings,
assigned owners, and planned response strategies. What document should the project manager create?
option1: Issue log
option2: Assumption log
option3: Risk register
option4: Lessons learned register
explanation:
The risk register is a project document that records details about
identified risks, including risk descriptions, categories, root causes,
probability and impact assessments, priority rankings based on scores,
assigned risk owners responsible for monitoring and response, planned
risk response strategies, and status of risk responses. The risk
register is created during the Identify Risks process and updated
throughout the project as new risks are identified, responses are
implemented, and risks are closed. It serves as the central repository
for all risk-related information and is a key tool for the Perform Risk
Analysis and Plan Risk Responses processes.
erro_alternative1:
The issue log tracks problems that have already occurred and require
resolution, not uncertain future events (risks) that may or may not
happen.
erro_alternative2:
The assumption log documents factors believed to be true for planning
purposes and constraints that limit options, which are related to but
distinct from risks.
erro_alternative4:
The lessons learned register captures knowledge gained throughout the
project about what worked well and what could be improved, serving as
organizational learning rather than risk tracking.

statement:
A project manager is evaluating whether to purchase insurance for
equipment valued at \$800,000. Risk analysis indicates there is a 15%
probability that the equipment could be damaged or lost during the
project. The insurance company quotes an annual premium of \$85,000 for
full coverage. What action should the project manager take regarding this insurance?
option1:
Reject the insurance and accept the risk since the expected loss is
greater than the premium
option2: Reject the insurance and implement risk mitigation measures instead
option3:
Purchase the insurance since the premium is less than the expected loss
value
option4:
Purchase the insurance since the premium is less than the expected loss
value
explanation:
The expected monetary value of the loss is calculated as: 15% ×
\$800,000 = \$120,000. When the insurance premium (\$85,000) is less
than the expected monetary value of the loss (\$120,000), purchasing
insurance represents a favorable risk transfer decision from a purely
financial perspective. The project pays \$85,000 to protect against a
potential \$800,000 loss that has an expected value of \$120,000,
resulting in a net benefit of \$35,000 compared to accepting the risk.
This calculation demonstrates that the cost of transferring the risk
through insurance is less than the expected cost of retaining the risk.
erro_alternative1:
Rejecting insurance when expected loss (\$120,000) exceeds the premium
(\$85,000) would mean accepting higher expected costs to avoid paying
lower insurance costs, which is financially unfavorable.
erro_alternative2:
While mitigation measures might reduce probability or impact, this
option doesn\'t recognize that the insurance premium is already
favorable compared to expected loss, and mitigation could complement
rather than replace insurance.
erro_alternative3:
Using an arbitrary percentage threshold (10% of equipment value =
\$80,000) ignores the proper analysis of expected monetary value versus
premium cost, as a premium slightly above 10% could still be favorable
if expected loss is higher.

statement:
A project manager needs to create a hierarchical chart that organizes
and categorizes all project resources by type, such as labor (by skill
or department), materials (by category), equipment (by type), and
supplies. This chart will help with resource planning and cost
estimation. What document should the project manager create?
option1: WBS
option2: RBS
option3: OBS
option4: RAM
explanation:
The Resource Breakdown Structure (RBS) is a hierarchical representation
of resources organized by category and type. The RBS typically starts
with broad resource categories (labor, materials, equipment, supplies)
and breaks them down into increasingly specific types. For example,
labor might be subdivided by department, skill level, or role; materials
by category and specific items; equipment by type and model. The RBS
helps with resource planning by ensuring all resource types are
identified, facilitates cost estimation by enabling resource-based
costing, and supports resource management by organizing resources in a
logical, hierarchical structure.
erro_alternative1:
WBS (Work Breakdown Structure) decomposes project deliverables and work
packages hierarchically but doesn\'t organize resources by type or
category.
erro_alternative3:
OBS (Organizational Breakdown Structure) shows the project organization
arranged by departments or reporting structure, not resource
categorization by type.
erro_alternative4:
RAM (Responsibility Assignment Matrix) shows which individuals or groups
are responsible for work packages using a matrix format, not a
hierarchical resource categorization.

statement:
A project manager has identified and analyzed several risks during risk
planning. For each risk, the project manager needs to assign an
individual who will be responsible for monitoring the risk, implementing
the planned response if the risk occurs, and reporting on risk status. What role is the project manager assigning for each risk?
option1: Risk analyst
option2: Risk owner
option3: Project sponsor
option4: Risk manager
explanation:
The risk owner is the person assigned responsibility for monitoring a
specific risk, implementing the planned risk response strategy if the
risk materializes, and reporting on the risk\'s status. Risk owners are
typically assigned during the Plan Risk Responses process and should
have the authority, knowledge, and resources needed to manage their
assigned risks effectively. Each identified risk should have a
designated owner who accepts accountability for that risk. The risk
owner is not necessarily responsible for all response actions but
ensures responses are executed and monitors whether the risk remains
within acceptable tolerance levels.
erro_alternative1:
Risk analyst is not a standard project role, though individuals may
perform risk analysis activities, but this doesn\'t describe the person
assigned ongoing responsibility for monitoring and responding to
specific risks.
erro_alternative3:
The project sponsor provides high-level support and funding but doesn\'t
typically own individual project risks, though they may be consulted on
risks exceeding the project manager\'s authority.
erro_alternative4:
Risk manager might be a role on large programs or in organizations with
dedicated risk management functions, but for individual project risks,
the assigned accountable person is the risk owner.

statement:
A project manager is working with a team that visualizes their workflow
on a board with columns representing different stages of work (To Do, In
Progress, Testing, Done). The team limits how many items can be in each
column simultaneously and continuously pulls new work from the backlog
as capacity becomes available rather than working in fixed iterations. What methodology is the team using?
option1: Scrum
option2: Kanban
option3: Waterfall
option4: XP
explanation:
Kanban is a visual workflow management method that uses a board to
represent work stages, implements work-in-progress (WIP) limits to
prevent overloading the system, and operates on a continuous flow model
where new work is pulled into the system as capacity becomes available
rather than in timeboxed iterations. Key Kanban principles include
visualizing work, limiting WIP to improve flow, managing flow by
monitoring cycle time and throughput, making process policies explicit,
and continuously improving. Unlike Scrum\'s fixed-length sprints, Kanban
allows work to move through the system continuously, making it
particularly effective for operational or support work with
unpredictable arrival patterns.
erro_alternative1:
Scrum uses timeboxed iterations (sprints) with defined ceremonies and
roles, rather than the continuous flow approach with WIP limits that
characterizes Kanban.
erro_alternative3:
Waterfall follows sequential phases where each phase must complete
before the next begins, not the continuous flow with visual boards and
WIP limits described.
erro_alternative4:
Extreme Programming (XP) is an Agile methodology focused on technical
practices like pair programming and test-driven development, typically
using iterations rather than continuous flow with WIP limits.

statement:
A project manager has identified 45 risks during a brainstorming session
with the team. Before developing detailed response strategies for all
risks, the project manager wants to prioritize them by assessing each
risk\'s probability of occurrence and potential impact on project
objectives, then categorizing them as high, medium, or low priority. What type of risk analysis should the project manager perform?
option1: Qualitative risk analysis
option2: Quantitative risk analysis
option3: Monte Carlo simulation
option4: Sensitivity analysis
explanation:
Qualitative risk analysis is the process of prioritizing identified
risks by assessing their probability of occurrence and impact on project
objectives using predefined scales (typically low, medium, high or
numerical scales like 1-5). This analysis is performed on all identified
risks to determine which require detailed quantitative analysis or
immediate response planning. Qualitative analysis is fast,
cost-effective, and helps prioritize risks by categorizing them into
risk levels (low, medium, high, very high) based on their probability
and impact scores. It typically uses tools like probability and impact
matrices to assign priority rankings that guide where to focus risk
management efforts.
erro_alternative2:
Quantitative risk analysis uses numerical techniques to evaluate the
probability and impact of risks with specific values rather than
categorical ratings, typically performed on high-priority risks after
qualitative analysis.
erro_alternative3:
Monte Carlo simulation is a specific quantitative risk analysis
technique that runs multiple iterations of probabilistic models to
assess overall project risk, not a method for initially categorizing
individual risks by priority.
erro_alternative4:
Sensitivity analysis is a quantitative technique that examines which
individual risks have the most potential impact on project outcomes,
used after qualitative analysis identifies high-priority risks, not for
initial prioritization.

statement:
A project manager is organizing identified risks during risk planning
and wants to group them systematically to ensure comprehensive risk
identification and facilitate efficient risk analysis. The project
manager decides to classify risks as technical risks, external risks,
organizational risks, or project management risks. What is the project manager defining when grouping risks this way?
option1: Risk priorities
option2: Risk categories
option3: Risk owners
option4: Risk responses
explanation:
Risk categories are groupings used to organize and classify project
risks systematically. Common categories include technical risks
(technology, requirements, complexity), external risks (regulatory,
market, customer), organizational risks (resources, funding,
priorities), and project management risks (planning, communication,
control). Risk categories are often structured hierarchically in a Risk
Breakdown Structure (RBS) and help ensure comprehensive risk
identification by prompting consideration of different risk sources.
Categorizing risks facilitates analysis by revealing patterns, enables
focused risk response strategies for similar risks, and supports risk
reporting by showing which categories contain the most threats.
erro_alternative1:
Risk priorities indicate which risks are most important based on their
probability and impact assessment, ranking risks as high, medium, or low
rather than organizing them by source or type.
erro_alternative3:
Risk owners are individuals assigned responsibility for monitoring
specific risks and implementing responses, representing accountability
rather than classification by type or source.
erro_alternative4:
Risk responses are the strategies developed to address risks (avoid,
mitigate, transfer, accept for threats; exploit, enhance, share, accept
for opportunities), not the classification system for organizing risks.

statement:
A project manager is considering using Monte Carlo analysis to assess
project schedule and cost risks. The team wants to understand how this
technique works before deciding whether to implement it. Which of the following statements is FALSE about Monte Carlo analysis?
option1:
Monte Carlo simulation runs hundreds or thousands of iterations using
probabilistic inputs
option2:
Monte Carlo analysis identifies which individual risk has the greatest
impact on the project
option3:
Monte Carlo simulation produces probability distributions showing
likelihood of different outcomes
option4:
Monte Carlo analysis uses three-point estimates for activity durations
or costs
explanation:
Monte Carlo analysis does not focus on identifying specific individual
risks or their impact. Instead, Monte Carlo simulation uses
probabilistic inputs to simulate potential project outcomes (e.g.,
schedule and cost uncertainties) and generates probability
distributions. It evaluates the overall variability and likelihood of
various project outcomes rather than isolating and analyzing individual
risks.

statement:
A project manager working with a Kanban team wants to ensure that
everyone---team members, stakeholders, and visitors---can quickly
understand the team\'s current work status, bottlenecks, and flow just
by glancing at a display in the team workspace. What should the project manager create to provide this highly visible,
real-time work status information?
option1: Information radiator
option2: Status report
option3: Gantt chart
option4: Risk register
explanation:
An information radiator is a highly visible, physical display that
provides real-time information about project or team status, placed in a
location where anyone can see it without needing to ask questions or
access systems. In Kanban contexts, this typically means a physical
board showing work items moving through workflow stages (To Do, In
Progress, Testing, Done) with WIP limits visible. Information radiators
can also include burndown charts, velocity metrics, impediment lists, or
other visual indicators. The term emphasizes that information
\"radiates\" from the display to anyone nearby, promoting transparency
and enabling quick status assessment without meetings or reports.
erro_alternative2:
Status reports are documents distributed to specific recipients on a
schedule, requiring people to actively read them rather than passively
receiving information from a visible display.
erro_alternative3:
Gantt charts show project schedules with task dependencies and timelines
but aren\'t typically used as highly visible, real-time displays in team
workspaces for continuous status awareness.
erro_alternative4:
Risk registers document identified risks and their characteristics but
don\'t provide the real-time workflow visualization and work status
information that information radiators display in team spaces.

statement:
A project manager is facilitating a problem-solving session where the
team needs to identify and explore the root causes of recurring quality
defects in their deliverables. The project manager wants to use a visual
tool that organizes potential causes into categories such as people,
processes, materials, equipment, environment, and management. What tool should the project manager use?
option1: Ishikawa diagram
option2: Pareto chart
option3: Control chart
option4: Scatter diagram
explanation:
An Ishikawa diagram (also called a fishbone diagram or cause-and-effect
diagram) is a visual tool used to systematically identify and organize
potential causes of a problem. The diagram resembles a fish skeleton,
with the problem statement at the \"head\" and major cause categories
forming the \"bones\" branching off the main spine. Common categories
include the 6Ms: Manpower (people), Methods (processes), Materials,
Machines (equipment), Measurements, and Mother Nature (environment). The
team brainstorms potential causes within each category, helping ensure
comprehensive analysis and revealing relationships between causes.
Ishikawa diagrams are particularly effective for root cause analysis
during quality problem-solving.
erro_alternative2:
Pareto charts rank problems or causes by frequency or impact in
descending order to identify the vital few causes contributing most to
problems, but don\'t organize causes into categories for root cause
exploration.
erro_alternative3:
Control charts monitor process performance over time by plotting data
points against control limits to identify trends or out-of-control
conditions, not for identifying root causes of problems.
erro_alternative4:
Scatter diagrams plot two variables to show whether correlation exists
between them, useful for analyzing relationships but not for
systematically organizing and categorizing potential causes of problems.

statement:
A project manager is assessing risks and needs to select an appropriate
rating scale for measuring risk impact. The team is debating between
using descriptive terms like \"very low, low, medium, high, very high\"
versus using a numerical scale like 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 where the intervals
between numbers have equal meaning and mathematical operations can be
performed. What type of scale represents the numerical approach with equal
intervals?
option1: Ordinal scale
option2: Nominal scale
option3: Interval scale
option4: Cardinal scale
explanation:
A cardinal scale (also called ratio scale in statistics) uses numerical
values where the intervals between numbers are equal and meaningful, and
mathematical operations like addition, subtraction, multiplication, and
division are valid. In risk management, using a cardinal scale (1, 2, 3,
4, 5) means the difference between 1 and 2 is the same as between 4 and
5, and you can calculate meaningful averages or perform probability ×
impact multiplications. This enables more precise quantitative risk
analysis compared to ordinal scales where the intervals may not be
equal. Cardinal scales are preferred when numerical analysis is needed
but require more precise calibration to ensure consistent interpretation
across evaluators.
erro_alternative1:
Ordinal scales rank items in order (like very low, low, medium, high,
very high) but the intervals between rankings aren\'t necessarily equal,
so mathematical operations aren\'t valid; you can\'t meaningfully
average \"low\" and \"high.\"
erro_alternative2:
Nominal scales categorize items into groups without any inherent order
or ranking (like risk categories: technical, external, organizational),
providing classification but no quantitative measurement.
erro_alternative3:
Interval scales have equal intervals but no true zero point (like
temperature in Celsius), while cardinal scales have both equal intervals
and a meaningful zero point enabling ratio comparisons.

statement:
A project manager is calculating the expected monetary value (EMV) of a
risk to determine whether to accept the risk or implement a mitigation
strategy. The calculation combines information about how likely the risk
is to occur with information about the consequences if it does occur.
option1:
The two dimensions of risk used to determine expected monetary value
are:
option2: Cost and schedule
option3: Severity and urgency
option4: Impact and priority
option5: Probability and impact
explanation:
Expected monetary value (EMV) is calculated by multiplying the
probability (likelihood) of a risk occurring by the impact (consequence)
if it occurs. For example, a risk with 20% probability of causing
\$50,000 in losses has an EMV of 0.20 × \$50,000 = \$10,000. These two
dimensions---probability and impact---are fundamental to risk assessment
across all risk management processes. Probability represents how likely
the risk event is to occur (expressed as percentage or decimal), while
impact represents the effect on project objectives if the risk
materializes (expressed as cost, time, scope, or quality effects). Both
dimensions must be assessed to prioritize risks and make informed
decisions about risk responses.
erro_alternative1:
Cost and schedule are project constraints that can be affected by risks,
but they don\'t represent the two dimensions used to calculate expected
value of individual risks.
erro_alternative2:
Severity and urgency are factors for prioritizing issues or risks in
some frameworks, but the standard dimensions for EMV calculation are
probability and impact, not these terms.
erro_alternative3:
Impact and priority are not independent dimensions---priority is
typically determined by combining probability and impact assessments, so
they don\'t represent the two input dimensions for EMV.

statement:
A project manager is managing risks for a construction project and
decides to purchase a performance bond and liability insurance to
protect against potential losses from contractor default and third-party
property damage claims. What risk response strategy is the project manager using?
option1: Risk mitigation
option2: Risk avoidance
option3: Risk acceptance
option4: Risk transfer
explanation:
Risk transfer shifts the financial impact and sometimes management
responsibility of a risk to a third party, typically through insurance,
bonds, warranties, guarantees, or contractual agreements. In this
scenario, purchasing insurance and bonds transfers the financial
consequences of risks to insurance companies and bonding agencies. While
the project still experiences the risk event if it occurs, the financial
burden is borne by the third party. Transfer usually involves paying a
premium or fee to the accepting party. Common transfer mechanisms
include fixed-price contracts (transferring cost risk to sellers),
insurance policies, performance bonds, and indemnification clauses.
Transfer doesn\'t eliminate the risk but shifts financial
responsibility.
erro_alternative1:
Risk mitigation reduces the probability or impact of a risk through
proactive actions like adding resources, improving processes, or
implementing controls, rather than shifting responsibility to third
parties.
erro_alternative2:
Risk avoidance eliminates the risk entirely by changing project plans to
avoid the risk condition, such as eliminating high-risk features or
choosing different approaches, not shifting consequences to others.
erro_alternative3:
Risk acceptance acknowledges the risk and decides to retain it without
taking proactive action, either because mitigation costs exceed
potential impact or the risk is within tolerance, rather than
transferring it.

statement:
A project manager has implemented risk response strategies for all
identified high-priority risks. After implementing these responses, some
level of risk exposure still remains even though the responses have
reduced the probability or impact. What are these remaining risks called after risk responses have been
implemented?
option1: Secondary risks
option2: Residual risks
option3: Contingent risks
option4: Accepted risks
explanation:
Residual risks are the risks that remain after risk response strategies
have been implemented. Even effective mitigation or transfer strategies
rarely eliminate risks entirely---they reduce probability or impact to
acceptable levels, but some exposure persists. For example, implementing
backup systems may reduce the probability of system failure from 30% to
5%, but the 5% residual risk remains. Residual risks should be
documented, monitored, and accepted by appropriate stakeholders. If
residual risks exceed acceptable tolerance levels after response
implementation, additional responses may be needed. Understanding
residual risk levels helps determine whether risk responses are adequate
or require enhancement.
erro_alternative1:
Secondary risks are new risks that arise as a direct result of
implementing risk responses, not the reduced version of the original
risk that persists after response implementation.
erro_alternative3:
Contingent risks (or conditional risks) are risks that will occur only
if certain trigger conditions are met, not simply the remaining exposure
after responses are applied.
erro_alternative4:
Accepted risks are risks where the decision has been made to acknowledge
and retain them without implementing response actions, whereas residual
risks remain after responses have been implemented.

statement:
When implementing Kanban development, which of the following should be
done?
option1: Establish fixed iteration timeboxes of two weeks
option2: Create the board on a whiteboard with erasable markers or physical cards
option3: Assign all work items to specific team members at the start
option4: Limit the product backlog to a maximum of 20 items
explanation:
Kanban boards are most effective when implemented as physical, tactile
displays using whiteboards with erasable markers, sticky notes, or index
cards that can be easily moved between columns. Physical boards provide
high visibility, encourage team collaboration around the board during
daily updates, make workflow and bottlenecks immediately obvious to
anyone walking by, and allow quick adjustments as the process evolves.
While digital Kanban tools exist and work for distributed teams,
physical boards are preferred when teams are co-located because they
serve as information radiators and focal points for team discussion. The
physical nature makes work tangible and workflow changes easy to
implement.
erro_alternative1:
Establishing fixed iteration timeboxes contradicts Kanban\'s continuous
flow model where work moves through the system as capacity becomes
available rather than in planned sprints.
erro_alternative3:
Kanban uses a pull system where team members pull work when they have
capacity rather than having work assigned upfront, enabling better flow
and preventing overallocation.
erro_alternative4:
Limiting the product backlog to a specific number isn\'t a Kanban
principle. WIP limits are applied to workflow columns (in progress,
testing) not to the backlog of future work.

statement:
A project manager has completed vendor selection, negotiated terms, and
is ready to establish a formal, legally binding agreement. Before the contract can be finalized and executed, what must the project
manager ensure is completed?
option1: Vendor performance evaluation criteria are established
option2: Change control procedures are implemented
option3: All terms are agreed upon and necessary approvals are obtained
option4: Lessons learned documentation is prepared
explanation:
Before finalizing a vendor contract, the project manager must ensure all
contractual terms have been negotiated and agreed upon by both parties,
including scope of work, deliverables, pricing, payment terms,
warranties, liabilities, dispute resolution procedures, and termination
clauses. Additionally, all necessary internal approvals must be obtained
from procurement, legal, finance, and senior management according to
organizational procurement policies and authority levels. Attempting to
finalize a contract without complete agreement or proper approvals can
result in invalid contracts, legal disputes, or violations of
organizational governance. Only after terms are settled and approvals
secured can authorized signatories execute the binding agreement.
erro_alternative1:
Vendor performance evaluation criteria should be established before
vendor selection to ensure objective evaluation, and are typically
defined earlier during the Conduct Procurements process, not at contract
finalization.
erro_alternative2:
Change control procedures are implemented after contract execution
during the Control Procurements process to manage contract
modifications, not as a prerequisite to finalizing the initial contract.
erro_alternative4:
Lessons learned documentation is prepared during project closing after
contract completion and vendor performance evaluation, not before the
contract is executed at the beginning of the procurement relationship.

statement: Which of the following is NOT true about a cost reimbursable contract?
option1: The buyer reimburses the seller for actual costs incurred plus a fee
option2: The buyer assumes most of the cost risk in the contract
option3:
The seller has maximum incentive to control costs and maximize
efficiency
option4:
This contract type is appropriate when scope is uncertain or difficult
to define
explanation:
This statement is NOT true because in cost reimbursable contracts, the
seller actually has minimal incentive to control costs since they are
reimbursed for all allowable costs regardless of efficiency. The seller
gets paid more when costs are higher, creating a potential conflict of
interest. This is why cost reimbursable contracts often include
incentive fees tied to performance targets or are structured as Cost
Plus Incentive Fee (CPIF) to motivate cost control. The buyer must
actively monitor costs and seller performance to prevent cost overruns.
In contrast, fixed-price contracts give sellers maximum incentive to
control costs because they keep any savings below the agreed price.

statement:
A project manager has negotiated a Cost Plus Incentive Fee (CPIF)
contract with the following terms: -   Target Cost: \$150,000 -   Target Fee: \$15,000 -   Actual Cost: \$163,000 -   Share Ratio: 70/30 (buyer/seller) What are the final fee and final price for this contract?
explanation:
In a CPIF contract, the final fee is adjusted based on cost performance
using the formula: Final Fee = Target Fee - (Seller\'s Share % × Cost
Overrun)
erro_alternative2:
\$15,000 and \$178,000 would result from not adjusting the fee for cost
overrun, ignoring the incentive structure entirely.
erro_alternative3:
\$18,100 and \$181,100 incorrectly adds the seller\'s overrun share to
the target fee rather than subtracting it, reversing the incentive
effect.
erro_alternative4:
\$13,000 and \$176,000 uses incorrect calculation methods that don\'t
properly apply the 70/30 share ratio to adjust the fee based on cost
performance.

statement: Which of the following is not a servant leadership principle?
option1: Removing impediments that block the team\'s progress
option2: Facilitating team collaboration and self-organization
option3: Creating an environment where team members can succeed
option4: Making decisions when the team can\'t agree
explanation:
Making decisions when the team can\'t agree contradicts servant
leadership principles, which emphasize facilitating consensus and
helping teams work through disagreements rather than imposing solutions.
When teams face conflict or disagreement, servant leaders facilitate
discussion to help the team reach their own decisions through
collaborative problem-solving, using techniques like ensuring all voices
are heard, exploring underlying interests, and helping the team find
integrative solutions. Stepping in to make decisions for the team
undermines self-organization, creates dependency on the leader, and
prevents the team from developing their own conflict resolution
capabilities. The servant leader\'s role is to guide the process, not
dictate the outcome.
erro_alternative1:
Removing impediments is a core servant leadership function where the
leader actively identifies and eliminates obstacles blocking the team\'s
progress, enabling them to focus on delivering value.
erro_alternative2:
Facilitating team collaboration and self-organization reflects the
servant leader\'s role in creating conditions for effective teamwork
while allowing the team to organize their own work and make decisions
collaboratively.
erro_alternative3:
Creating an environment where team members can succeed demonstrates the
servant leader\'s focus on supporting others\' growth and performance by
providing resources, removing barriers, and fostering psychological
safety.

statement:
A project manager needs to evaluate and select a vendor from 15
qualified bidders for a complex IT implementation project. The project
manager wants to use a systematic approach that weights evaluation
criteria based on importance and scores each vendor against those
criteria to identify the best fit. What vendor selection technique should the project manager use?
option1: Weighted scoring system
option2: Independent estimates
option3: Proposal evaluation
option4: Screening system
explanation:
A weighted scoring system (also called weighted scoring model or
multi-criteria decision analysis) assigns relative importance values to
evaluation criteria, then scores each vendor against each criterion,
multiplies scores by importance values, and totals the results to rank
vendors objectively. For example, technical capability might receive an
importance value of 40%, price 30%, past performance 20%, and delivery
schedule 10%. Each vendor receives scores (like 1-5 or 1-10) for each
criterion, which are multiplied by importance values and summed. This
systematic approach enables transparent, consistent evaluation across
multiple criteria and helps justify selection decisions to stakeholders
by showing how vendors were rated against defined standards.
erro_alternative2:
Independent estimates involve the buyer creating their own cost or
schedule estimates to compare against vendor proposals for
reasonableness, helping detect unrealistic bids but not providing
comprehensive multi-criteria evaluation.
erro_alternative3:
Proposal evaluation is the general process of reviewing vendor proposals
against requirements, but doesn\'t specifically describe the systematic
methodology for comparing vendors across multiple criteria.
erro_alternative4:
Screening systems use minimum requirements to filter out unqualified
vendors early in the process, reducing the pool to qualified candidates,
but don\'t provide the detailed comparative analysis needed for final
selection.

statement:
A project manager is reviewing performance feedback and notices they
excel at creating detailed schedules, managing budgets, and tracking
project metrics. However, stakeholders mention the project manager
struggles with building relationships, motivating team members, and
facilitating productive discussions during conflicts. Which category of skills does the project manager need to develop?
option1: Technical skills
option2: Hard skills
option3: Domain expertise
option4: Soft skills
explanation:
Soft skills (also called interpersonal skills, people skills, or
emotional intelligence) include communication, leadership, motivation,
conflict resolution, negotiation, team building, empathy, active
listening, and relationship management. These skills focus on how people
interact and work together rather than technical or methodological
competencies. The scenario describes deficiencies in relationship
building, motivation, and conflict facilitation---all soft skills. While
hard skills like scheduling and budgeting are important for project
execution, soft skills are often more critical for project success
because projects depend on people working effectively together. Research
consistently shows that soft skills distinguish successful project
managers from unsuccessful ones.
erro_alternative1:
Technical skills refer to specialized knowledge in specific domains like
software development, engineering, or construction, which isn\'t what\'s
described in the feedback about interpersonal challenges.
erro_alternative2:
Hard skills are teachable, measurable abilities like project scheduling,
budgeting, and quantitative analysis---areas where the project manager
already excels according to the scenario.
erro_alternative3:
Domain expertise refers to specialized knowledge in particular
industries or fields (healthcare, finance, manufacturing) rather than
the interpersonal competencies needed for relationship building and
motivation.

statement:
A project manager is facing a disagreement between the development team
and the client about whether a deliverable is complete. The team
believes they\'ve finished all required work and met specifications,
while the client claims several features are missing or don\'t function
as expected. What project document should have detailed the acceptance criteria to
prevent this disagreement?
option1: Resource breakdown structure
option2: Risk register
option3: Communications management plan
option4: WBS dictionary
explanation:
The WBS dictionary provides detailed information about each work package
in the WBS, including descriptions of work, deliverables, acceptance
criteria, required resources, cost estimates, quality requirements, and
responsible assignments. Acceptance criteria documented in the WBS
dictionary define the specific conditions that must be satisfied for
deliverables to be accepted, establishing objective standards agreed
upon by both team and client before work begins. By documenting
acceptance criteria in the WBS dictionary during scope planning, the
project manager creates a reference that prevents disputes about what
constitutes complete, acceptable work. The WBS dictionary serves as the
detailed companion to the WBS, providing the specificity needed to
prevent misunderstandings.
erro_alternative1:
Resource breakdown structure organizes project resources by category and
type but doesn\'t define acceptance criteria or establish standards for
determining whether deliverables meet expectations.
erro_alternative2:
Risk register documents identified risks, their characteristics, and
planned responses but doesn\'t establish the specific conditions and
standards that determine whether deliverables are acceptable.
erro_alternative3:
Communications management plan defines how project information will be
shared, distributed, and managed but doesn\'t document the detailed
acceptance criteria needed to evaluate deliverable completeness
objectively.

statement:
A project manager wants to verify that project deliverables conform to
requirements and quality standards, and also ensure that the team is
following established quality processes and procedures correctly
throughout execution. What quality management activities should the project manager conduct?
option1: Risk assessments and stakeholder analysis
option2: Variance analysis and forecasting
option3: Inspections and audits
option4: Brainstorming and affinity diagrams
explanation:
Inspections and audits are complementary quality management activities
that serve different purposes. Inspections examine deliverables,
products, or work results to verify they meet requirements,
specifications, and quality standards---this is part of Control Quality
focused on deliverable conformance. Audits are structured reviews that
examine whether quality processes and procedures are being followed
correctly and identify opportunities for improvement---this is part of
Manage Quality Assurance focused on process conformance. Together,
inspections verify \"what\" is produced while audits verify \"how\"
it\'s produced. Both activities are essential for comprehensive quality
management, ensuring both deliverable quality and process effectiveness.
erro_alternative1:
Risk assessments and stakeholder analysis are planning activities
focused on identifying risks and understanding stakeholder needs, not
verifying deliverable conformance or process adherence during execution.
erro_alternative2:
Variance analysis and forecasting are monitoring techniques that compare
actual performance against baselines and project future outcomes, but
don\'t specifically verify quality or process conformance.
erro_alternative4:
Brainstorming and affinity diagrams are idea generation and organization
techniques used during planning or problem-solving, not verification
activities for deliverables or process compliance.

statement:
Which of the following is NOT true about the Close Project or Phase
process and the Control Procurements process?
option1:
Close Project or Phase closes out the entire project or phase, while
Control Procurements manages individual procurement contracts
option2: Control Procurements occurs only during the Closing Focus Area
option3: Both processes involve obtaining formal acceptance of deliverables
option4:
Close Project or Phase includes documenting lessons learned and
archiving information
explanation:
This statement is NOT true because Control Procurements occurs
throughout project execution and monitoring, not only during closing.
Control Procurements is the process of managing procurement
relationships, monitoring vendor performance, ensuring deliverables meet
requirements, processing payments, and managing contract changes during
the execution phase. While Control Procurements does include closing
individual contracts (which happens during the Closing Focus Area), the
majority of procurement control activities occur during execution as
vendors perform work. In contrast, Close Project or Phase specifically
occurs in the Closing Focus Area to finalize all activities and formally
close the project or phase.

statement:
A project manager has contracted with a general contractor for
construction work. The general contractor has hired several
subcontractors to perform specialized tasks. When a subcontractor\'s
work causes delays, the project manager attempts to take legal action
directly against the subcontractor. What legal principle prevents the project manager from having a direct
contractual relationship with the subcontractor?
option1: Indemnification
option2: Force majeure
option3: Warranties
option4: Privity
explanation:
Privity (or privity of contract) is the legal principle that establishes
a direct contractual relationship exists only between the parties who
signed the contract. In this scenario, the project manager has privity
only with the general contractor, and the general contractor has privity
with the subcontractors---but no direct privity exists between the
project manager and subcontractors. This means the project manager
cannot directly enforce contractual obligations against subcontractors
or take legal action against them, even if their work affects the
project. The project manager must work through the general contractor to
address subcontractor performance issues. Understanding privity is
important for managing procurement relationships and knowing which
parties can enforce contractual terms.
erro_alternative1:
Indemnification is a contractual provision where one party agrees to
compensate another for losses or damages, not the legal principle
governing direct contractual relationships between parties.
erro_alternative2:
Force majeure refers to contract clauses that excuse performance when
extraordinary events beyond parties\' control occur, not the principle
determining who has contractual relationships with whom.
erro_alternative3:
Warranties are promises or guarantees about product quality,
performance, or characteristics, not the legal concept defining direct
contractual relationships between specific parties.

statement:
The risk associated with performance specifications is usually the
responsibility of the:
option1: Project manager
option2: Customer
option3: Project sponsor
option4: Seller
explanation:
When contracts use performance specifications (also called functional
specifications), the seller assumes the risk of determining how to meet
the specified performance requirements. The buyer defines what results
are required (output, capacity, efficiency, reliability) but not how to
achieve them, giving the seller flexibility in design and implementation
approaches. If the seller\'s chosen approach fails to meet performance
specifications, the seller bears responsibility for redesign, rework, or
replacement at their own cost. This contrasts with design specifications
where the buyer specifies exactly how work should be performed, placing
more risk on the buyer if the prescribed approach doesn\'t achieve
desired results. Performance specifications transfer technical risk to
sellers who have specialized expertise.
erro_alternative1:
The project manager coordinates and oversees the contract but doesn\'t
assume the technical risk of meeting performance specifications, which
lies with the party responsible for delivery.
erro_alternative2:
The customer (buyer) establishes what performance is required but
transfers the risk of how to achieve it to the seller, retaining only
the risk of defining appropriate performance targets.
erro_alternative3:
The project sponsor provides funding and strategic direction but
doesn\'t assume technical responsibility for the seller\'s ability to
meet contractually specified performance levels.

statement:
A project manager has completed procurement planning, developed bid
documents, and is now obtaining proposals from potential vendors,
evaluating their submissions against selection criteria, selecting the
best-qualified vendor, and negotiating contract terms. What procurement process is the project manager performing?
option1: Plan Sourcing Strategy
option2: Conduct Procurements
option3: Control Procurements
option4: Close Procurements
explanation:
Conduct Procurements is the process of obtaining seller responses to bid
documents, evaluating proposals or bids, selecting qualified sellers,
and negotiating and awarding contracts. This process occurs in the
Executing Focus Area after procurement planning is complete. Key
activities include advertising procurement opportunities, holding bidder
conferences, receiving and evaluating proposals using selection
criteria, conducting negotiations with preferred sellers, and executing
formal contracts. Conduct Procurements transforms the procurement
management plan into actual vendor relationships and signed contracts
that authorize sellers to begin work, establishing the legal agreements
that govern procurement execution.
erro_alternative1:
Plan Sourcing Strategy occurs during planning to determine make-or-buy
decisions, identify procurement needs, develop the procurement approach,
and create bid documents, all completed before soliciting vendor
proposals.
erro_alternative3:
Control Procurements manages ongoing procurement relationships after
contracts are awarded, monitoring vendor performance, ensuring
deliverables meet requirements, processing payments, and managing
contract changes during execution.
erro_alternative4:
Close Procurements finalizes and closes individual contracts after work
is completed, obtaining final acceptance, settling claims, updating
records, and releasing vendors, occurring at the end of contracts.

statement:
A project manager needs to select a contract type for a construction
project with well-defined scope, detailed specifications, and minimal
expected changes. The organization wants maximum cost certainty and is
willing to pay a premium to transfer cost risk to the contractor. What contract type should the project manager recommend?
option1: Fixed price
option2: Cost reimbursable
option3: Time and materials
option4: Cost plus incentive fee
explanation:
Fixed price contracts (also called firm fixed price or lump sum
contracts) establish a total price for defined scope that doesn\'t
change regardless of the seller\'s actual costs. The seller assumes all
cost risk. They profit if actual costs are below the fixed price but
lose money if costs exceed it. This contract type provides maximum cost
certainty for buyers, making budgeting and financial planning
straightforward. Fixed price contracts are most appropriate when scope
is well-defined and stable, requirements are clear and unlikely to
change, and the seller has sufficient information to estimate
accurately. Sellers typically build contingency into fixed prices to
cover risk, so buyers may pay a premium for cost certainty.
erro_alternative2:
Cost reimbursable contracts reimburse sellers for actual costs plus a
fee, placing cost risk on the buyer rather than providing the cost
certainty and risk transfer the organization wants.
erro_alternative3:
Time and materials contracts pay based on hours worked and materials
consumed, creating cost uncertainty since final costs depend on effort
required, not providing the cost predictability desired.
erro_alternative4:
Cost plus incentive fee is a specific type of cost reimbursable contract
that still places significant cost risk on the buyer through cost
reimbursement, though incentives encourage cost control.

statement:
A project manager is managing a contract dispute with a vendor where
both parties cannot reach agreement through negotiation or mediation.
The contract includes a clause requiring that disputes be resolved
through a binding process where a neutral third party hears both sides
and makes a final decision that both parties must accept. What dispute resolution method is specified in the contract?
option1: Litigation
option2: Mediation
option3: Arbitration
option4: Negotiation
explanation:
Arbitration is a dispute resolution method where a neutral third party
(arbitrator or arbitration panel) hears evidence and arguments from both
sides and renders a binding decision that both parties must accept.
Unlike mediation where the third party facilitates agreement between
parties, the arbitrator has authority to impose a solution. Arbitration
is typically faster and less expensive than litigation, conducted
privately rather than in public courts, and often included in contracts
as the required dispute resolution method. The arbitrator\'s decision is
legally enforceable and generally cannot be appealed except in cases of
fraud or arbitrator misconduct. Many contracts specify arbitration to
avoid lengthy court proceedings.
erro_alternative1:
Litigation involves resolving disputes through formal court proceedings
with judges or juries, which is more public, expensive, and
time-consuming than arbitration, though it does provide binding
decisions.
erro_alternative2:
Mediation uses a neutral facilitator to help parties reach their own
mutually acceptable agreement, but the mediator doesn\'t impose
decisions or make binding rulings like arbitrators do.
erro_alternative4:
Negotiation involves direct discussions between the disputing parties to
reach agreement without third-party involvement, which the scenario
indicates has already been attempted unsuccessfully.

statement:
A project manager is negotiating a contract for research and development
work where the scope is highly uncertain and difficult to define
upfront. The organization wants the seller to be reimbursed for all
allowable costs and receive an additional fee that remains constant
regardless of the seller\'s cost performance. What contract type should the project manager use?
option1: Cost Plus Percentage of Cost (CPPC)
option2: Cost Plus Fixed Fee (CPFF)
option3: Cost Plus Incentive Fee (CPIF)
option4: Firm Fixed Price (FFP)
explanation:
Cost Plus Percentage of Cost (CPPC) contracts reimburse the seller for
all allowable costs and pay a fee calculated as a percentage of those
actual costs. For example, if the contract specifies 10% fee and actual
costs are \$100,000, the seller receives \$110,000 total. However, CPPC
contracts are generally discouraged and often prohibited for government
work because they create perverse incentives. The higher the seller\'s
costs, the higher their fee, so sellers have financial motivation to
increase rather than control costs. This misalignment makes CPPC the
least desirable cost reimbursable contract type, though it may
occasionally be used when no other arrangement is feasible.
erro_alternative2:
Cost Plus Fixed Fee (CPFF) provides a fixed fee amount that doesn\'t
change based on costs, which is what the scenario describes wanting
rather than CPPC where the fee varies with costs.
erro_alternative3:
Cost Plus Incentive Fee (CPIF) adjusts the fee based on cost performance
against targets using a share ratio, providing incentives for cost
control rather than the constant fee described.
erro_alternative4:
Firm Fixed Price (FFP) establishes a total price for defined scope,
inappropriate for highly uncertain work and doesn\'t provide cost
reimbursement or separate fees as described.

statement:
A project manager is implementing Lean principles and training the team
to identify and eliminate the seven wastes in their project processes.
The team is learning to distinguish between activities that add value
and those that represent waste. Which of the following is NOT considered waste on a project?
option1: Waiting for approvals or information before work can proceed
option2: Producing more documentation than stakeholders actually need or use
option3: Regular preventive maintenance on critical equipment
option4: Rework caused by defects or errors in previous work
explanation:
Regular preventive maintenance is not waste because it prevents larger
failures, downtime, and costly emergency repairs that would disrupt the
project. While preventive maintenance takes time and resources when
equipment is currently functioning, it\'s a value-protecting activity
that reduces overall project risk and prevents the much greater waste of
unplanned equipment failures during critical project phases. Preventive
maintenance is similar to quality assurance activities that prevent
problems rather than fix them after occurrence. The seven wastes focus
on non-value-added activities, but preventive actions that avoid future
problems are considered prudent investments, not waste.
erro_alternative1:
Waiting for approvals or information represents waste because no value
is being created during delay periods, and reducing wait times through
process improvements can significantly accelerate delivery.
erro_alternative2:
Producing excessive documentation that stakeholders don\'t need or use
represents overprocessing waste, consuming resources on outputs that
don\'t add value to customers or the project.
erro_alternative4:
Rework caused by defects represents waste because resources are consumed
fixing problems rather than creating new value, and preventing defects
through quality processes eliminates this waste.

statement:
A project manager is preparing to perform the Monitor Stakeholder
Engagement process to assess the effectiveness of stakeholder engagement
strategies and identify any adjustments needed to improve stakeholder
relationships and support. Which of the following is an important input to the Monitor Stakeholder
Engagement process?
option1: Stakeholder register
option2: Communications management plan
option3: Lessons learned register
option4: Stakeholder engagement plan
explanation:
The stakeholder engagement plan is a critical input to Monitor
Stakeholder Engagement because it defines the strategies and approaches
for engaging stakeholders based on their needs, expectations, and
influence levels. The process monitors how well these planned engagement
strategies are working by comparing current stakeholder engagement
levels against desired levels documented in the plan. Without the
stakeholder engagement plan as a baseline reference, the project manager
cannot assess whether engagement efforts are effective or determine what
adjustments are needed. The plan provides the comparison point for
evaluating whether stakeholders are moving toward desired engagement
levels (unaware, resistant, neutral, supportive, leading).
erro_alternative1:
The stakeholder register identifies stakeholders and their
characteristics but doesn\'t contain the engagement strategies and
desired engagement levels that serve as the baseline for monitoring
engagement effectiveness.
erro_alternative2:
The communications management plan defines how project information will
be shared but doesn\'t specifically contain the stakeholder engagement
strategies, current versus desired engagement levels, and tailored
approaches that are monitored.
erro_alternative3:
The lessons learned register captures knowledge from the project but is
updated based on monitoring results rather than serving as input that
defines what engagement approaches should be assessed.

statement:
A project manager needs to prioritize stakeholder engagement efforts by
analyzing which stakeholders require the most attention. The project
manager wants to use a model that categorizes stakeholders based on
three attributes: their power to influence the project, the urgency of
their claims on the project, and the legitimacy of their involvement. What stakeholder analysis model should the project manager use?
option1: Power/interest grid
option2: Salience model
option3: Power/influence grid
option4: Stakeholder cube
explanation:
The salience model categorizes stakeholders based on three attributes:
power (ability to impose their will), urgency (need for immediate
attention), and legitimacy (appropriateness of their involvement).
Stakeholders possessing all three attributes are considered \"definitive
stakeholders\" requiring highest priority attention. Those with two
attributes are \"expectant stakeholders\" (dangerous, dependent, or
dominant), and those with only one attribute are \"latent stakeholders\"
(dormant, discretionary, or demanding). The model helps project managers
prioritize stakeholder engagement by identifying which stakeholders are
most salient (prominent or important) based on these combined
characteristics, enabling strategic allocation of engagement efforts.
erro_alternative1:
The power/interest grid categorizes stakeholders based on only two
dimensions (power and interest level) and doesn\'t incorporate urgency
or legitimacy attributes central to the salience model.
erro_alternative3:
The influence/impact grid assesses stakeholders based on their influence
over project decisions and potential impact on outcomes but doesn\'t use
the three-attribute framework of power, urgency, and legitimacy.
erro_alternative4:
The stakeholder cube is a three-dimensional model but typically uses
different attributes (power, interest, attitude or other combinations)
rather than the specific salience model attributes of power, urgency,
and legitimacy.

statement:
A project manager is beginning to develop strategies for engaging
stakeholders effectively throughout the project. The project manager
needs to understand who the stakeholders are, their characteristics, and
their relationship to the project before determining appropriate
engagement approaches. Which of the following is an input to the Plan Stakeholder Engagement
process?
option1: Stakeholder engagement plan
option2: Stakeholder register
option3: Work performance reports
option4: Change requests
explanation:
The stakeholder register is a critical input to Plan Stakeholder
Engagement because it contains identification and assessment information
about all project stakeholders, including their names, positions,
influence levels, interests, expectations, and potential impact on the
project. The Plan Stakeholder Engagement process uses this stakeholder
information to develop appropriate strategies for engaging each
stakeholder or stakeholder group based on their characteristics. Without
the stakeholder register providing detailed stakeholder information, the
project manager cannot develop tailored engagement strategies that
address specific stakeholder needs, influence levels, and expectations.
The register provides the foundation for all stakeholder engagement
planning.
erro_alternative1:
The stakeholder engagement plan is an output of Plan Stakeholder
Engagement, not an input, as it is the document being created through
this process.
erro_alternative3:
Work performance reports provide information about project performance
during execution and are used in monitoring processes, not as input for
planning stakeholder engagement strategies.
erro_alternative4:
Change requests are typically outputs when modifications are identified
during execution or monitoring processes, not inputs used to initially
plan stakeholder engagement approaches.

statement:
A project manager is working to build an effective team by facilitating
conflict resolution, motivating team members, providing coaching and
mentoring, and using emotional intelligence to navigate team dynamics
and stakeholder relationships. What category of project management skills is the project manager
demonstrating?
option1: Interpersonal and Team Skills
option2: Technical project management skills
option3: Strategic and business management skills
option4: Process improvement skills
explanation:
Interpersonal and team skills (also called soft skills or people skills)
include the abilities to work effectively with others, such as conflict
management, emotional intelligence, influencing, motivation,
negotiation, team building, coaching, political and cultural awareness,
and leadership. These skills focus on human interactions and
relationships rather than technical methodologies or business strategy.
The PMI Talent Triangle identifies three skill categories: technical
project management (planning, scheduling, risk management), leadership
(interpersonal and team skills), and strategic and business management
(business acumen and strategy). Interpersonal and team skills are often
the most critical for project success because projects depend
fundamentally on people working together effectively.
erro_alternative2:
Technical project management skills involve planning, scheduling, risk
management, quality management, and other methodology-based competencies
rather than the people-focused skills described.
erro_alternative3:
Strategic and business management skills include understanding
organizational strategy, benefits realization, market awareness, and
business value delivery rather than interpersonal relationship
management.
erro_alternative4:
Process improvement skills focus on analyzing and enhancing work
processes through techniques like Lean or Six Sigma rather than the team
dynamics and relationship skills described.

statement:
A project selection committee is evaluating three potential projects and
wants to use a financial metric that calculates the discount rate at
which the net present value of all cash flows equals zero. This metric
helps compare the profitability of different investment options. What financial metric should the committee calculate?
option1: Net present value (NPV)
option2: Payback period
option3: Benefit-cost ratio (BCR)
option4: Internal rate of return (IRR)
explanation:
Internal rate of return (IRR) is the discount rate at which the net
present value (NPV) of all project cash flows equals zero. It represents
the project\'s expected rate of return and can be compared against a
company\'s required rate of return or cost of capital to determine
project viability. Projects with IRR exceeding the required rate of
return are generally acceptable investments. IRR enables comparison of
projects with different scales and durations by expressing returns as
percentages rather than absolute dollar amounts. Higher IRR indicates
more profitable investments. The IRR calculation requires iterative
computation and is typically performed using financial calculators or
spreadsheet software.
erro_alternative1:
Net present value (NPV) calculates the present value of future cash
flows minus initial investment, expressed as a dollar amount rather than
the discount rate that makes NPV equal zero.
erro_alternative2:
Payback period measures how long it takes to recover the initial
investment but doesn\'t calculate discount rates or account for the time
value of money beyond the payback point.
erro_alternative3:
Benefit-cost ratio (BCR) divides total benefits by total costs to show
the return per dollar invested but doesn\'t express this as the discount
rate that zeros out NPV.

statement:
Just as the project manager begins planning for a high-profile
technology upgrade, a key supplier informs them that a critical software
component will be delayed due to unforeseen development challenges. This
delay threatens to derail the project\'s tight schedule and could lead
to significant disruptions. What should the project manager do next to minimize the impact on the
project schedule?
option1: Record the delay in the risk register
option2: Initiate a risk analysis to explore the implications of the delay
option3:
Document the delay in the issue log and assess impact mitigation
strategies
option4: Follow the plan of action laid out in the risk management plan
explanation:
The supplier delay is a current problem that has already occurred,
making it an issue rather than a risk. The issue log is the appropriate
place to document this confirmed delay and track mitigation strategies
that minimize schedule impact. Recording the issue establishes
accountability for resolution and enables the project manager to explore
alternatives like parallel development, alternative suppliers, or scope
adjustments to protect the tight schedule.
erro_alternative1:
Recording in the risk register is inappropriate because the delay has
already been confirmed by the supplier, transitioning it from uncertain
future event (risk) to current problem (issue) requiring immediate
mitigation rather than probabilistic assessment.
erro_alternative2:
Initiating risk analysis treats a confirmed delay as uncertain when the
supplier has definitively stated the problem exists, and analysis
without first documenting the issue formally delays the mitigation
planning needed to minimize schedule impact.
erro_alternative4:
Following the risk management plan is too vague when specific immediate
action is needed to document and address a confirmed supplier delay
threatening critical project timelines during the planning phase.

statement:
Halfway through the construction of a new commercial building, the
project manager discovers that a critical shipment of materials will be
delayed due to supply chain issues. This delay poses a significant risk
to the project\'s schedule, potentially pushing back the completion
date. What is the first course of action for the project manager to mitigate
this risk?
option1:
Order materials from a more expensive, immediate source to stay on
schedule
option2:
Use the risk management plan to determine the preplanned course of
action
option3: Hold a team meeting to brainstorm potential solutions to the delay
option4: Negotiate with the client for an extension of the project deadline
explanation:
The risk management plan should contain predetermined response
strategies for identified risks, including common construction risks
like material supply chain delays. Consulting this plan first ensures
the project manager follows the agreed-upon approach that stakeholders
approved during planning, which likely includes specific mitigation
strategies, contingency plans, or fallback options. This systematic
approach prevents reactive decision-making and ensures responses align
with planned risk thresholds and budgets.
erro_alternative1:
Ordering from an expensive immediate source jumps to a specific solution
without first checking whether this risk was anticipated and what
response strategies were already planned, potentially wasting money if
better alternatives exist in the plan.
erro_alternative3:
Holding a team meeting to brainstorm treats this as an unexpected
problem requiring new solutions when material delays are foreseeable
construction risks that should have planned responses in the risk
management plan.
erro_alternative4:
Negotiating for deadline extension is premature without first attempting
the mitigation strategies that should exist in the risk management plan,
and may be unnecessary if planned responses can maintain the schedule.

statement:
As the project manager for a new aerospace manufacturing facility,
you\'re in the process of setting up the project\'s risk management
procedures. You recognize the need for a structured approach to identify
and categorize the vast array of risks associated with such a complex
project, from supply chain disruptions to technical failures and
regulatory compliance issues. What is the most effective way to organize these risks to ensure a
comprehensive yet manageable risk management process?
option1: Develop a detailed risk register that lists all identified risks
option2:
Use a risk breakdown structure (RBS) to categorize risks into clearly
defined groups
option3: Create one category for external risks and another for internal risks
option4:
Assign each project team member the responsibility for managing a
specific type of risk
explanation:
A Risk Breakdown Structure provides a hierarchical framework that
organizes risks into categories and subcategories, making the vast array
of risks in a complex aerospace project both comprehensive and
manageable. The RBS enables systematic identification by ensuring all
risk categories are considered, facilitates focused analysis by grouping
related risks, and supports effective communication by providing a
common taxonomy. For aerospace projects, typical RBS categories might
include technical risks, organizational risks, external risks, and
project management risks, each with relevant subcategories.
erro_alternative1:
Developing a detailed risk register is essential for documenting
identified risks but doesn\'t provide the organizational structure for
categorizing them, as the register is a list format tool that benefits
from the RBS\'s categorization framework.
erro_alternative3:
Creating only two categories (external and internal) is overly
simplistic for a complex aerospace manufacturing project and doesn\'t
provide the granularity needed to systematically identify and manage
diverse risks across supply chain, technical, regulatory, and other
dimensions.
erro_alternative4:
Assigning team members responsibility for specific risk types addresses
risk ownership rather than risk organization, and ownership assignments
work better after risks are properly categorized through a structure
like the RBS.

statement:
In the early stages of a new mobile application development project, the
project manager identifies a significant risk: potential delays in the
development phase due to the integration of novel, untested
technologies. Aware that such delays could subsequently postpone
critical tasks like beta testing, user feedback collection, and the
launch of marketing initiatives, the project manager seeks to
proactively develop mitigation strategies. What is the most effective mitigation strategy for the project manager
to implement to address the risk of development delays?
option1:
Pre-allocate additional time buffers in the project schedule for
high-risk tasks
option2:
Outsource the development of the most complex features to specialized
firms
option3:
Conduct a preliminary risk assessment solely focused on the technology
integration phase
option4:
Increase the frequency of project status meetings during the development
phase
explanation:
Pre-allocating time buffers (schedule contingency reserves) specifically
for high-risk technology integration tasks directly mitigates the impact
of potential delays by building flexibility into the schedule. This
approach ensures that if delays occur during the integration of untested
technologies, the buffers absorb the impact without cascading to
dependent activities like beta testing and marketing launches. The
targeted allocation focuses contingency where risk is highest rather
than padding the entire schedule uniformly.
erro_alternative2:
Outsourcing complex features to specialized firms might reduce technical
