the detailed network analysis and critical path calculation that CPM
offers.

statement:
A project manager recently took over a project from a senior project
manager who left the company. The new project manager found out that
several team members are lacking critical skills to carry out project
activities. The previous project manager also mentioned that many of the
team members will need specialized training. Which of the following actions should the project manager take next to
identify the appropriate training for the team members?
option1: Conduct a skills assessment of all team members immediately
option2: Request additional experienced resources from functional managers
option3: Hire external consultants to fill the skill gaps
option4: Refer to the Resource Management Plan
explanation:
The Resource Management Plan (part of the project management plan)
documents how project resources will be acquired, developed, managed,
and released, including training needs, skill development approaches,
team development activities, and recognition and rewards strategies.
Since the previous project manager mentioned training needs, these
should be documented in the Resource Management Plan along with planned
approaches for addressing skill gaps. By reviewing this plan first, the
project manager can understand what training was already identified,
planned, and potentially budgeted for before taking additional actions.
This ensures continuity with previous planning, leverages work already
completed, and provides context for skill development needs before
conducting assessments or requesting additional resources.
erro_alternative1:
Conducting skills assessments before reviewing existing planning
documentation may duplicate work already completed and documented in the
Resource Management Plan by the previous project manager.
erro_alternative2:
Requesting additional experienced resources assumes replacement is
better than development and ignores training plans that may already
exist in the Resource Management Plan.
erro_alternative3:
Hiring external consultants is expensive and may be unnecessary if the
Resource Management Plan already documents training approaches and
budgets for developing existing team members\' skills.

statement: The agile manifesto contains how many guiding principles?
explanation:
The Agile Manifesto contains 12 guiding principles that support and
elaborate on the four values stated in the manifesto. These 12
principles provide specific guidance on topics including customer
satisfaction through early and continuous delivery, welcoming changing
requirements, delivering working software frequently, collaboration
between business people and developers, building projects around
motivated individuals, face-to-face conversation, working software as
the primary measure of progress, sustainable development pace,
continuous attention to technical excellence, simplicity,
self-organizing teams, and regular reflection on effectiveness. By
reviewing all 12 principles, the team can make informed decisions about
how to adapt their hybrid approach, understand the foundational thinking
behind agile practices, and ensure their process improvements align with
core agile values and principles rather than just adopting agile
ceremonies superficially.
erro_alternative1:
4 represents the number of ***values*** in the Agile Manifesto, not the
number of ***guiding principles***.

statement:
A project manager is reviewing a Cost Plus Incentive Fee (CPIF) contract
with a vendor for a critical project deliverable. The contract has an
estimated cost of \$250,000 with a predetermined fee of \$15,000. The
contract includes a sharing ratio of 75/25 (buyer/seller) if the seller
can reduce costs below the estimate. The contract also stipulates a
maximum fee of \$24,000 and a minimum fee of \$8,000. The vendor has
completed the work, and the actual cost came in at \$225,000. What will be the final fee for the seller and the total savings for the
buyer?
explanation:
In a CPIF contract, when actual costs are less than estimated costs, the
cost savings are shared between buyer and seller according to the
predetermined sharing ratio. First, calculate the cost savings:
\$250,000 (estimated) - \$225,000 (actual) = \$25,000 in savings. Next,
apply the 75/25 sharing ratio: the seller receives 25% of the savings
(\$25,000 × 0.25 = \$6,250) while the buyer receives 75% of the savings
(\$25,000 × 0.75 = \$18,750). The seller\'s final fee equals the
predetermined fee plus their share of savings: \$15,000 + \$6,250 =
\$21,250. This final fee of \$21,250 falls within the contract\'s fee
range (\$8,000 minimum to \$24,000 maximum), so no adjustment is needed.
The buyer saves their 75% share of the cost reduction (\$18,750) while
still paying the seller an incentive for efficient cost management,
creating a win-win outcome that encourages vendor cost control while
ensuring quality delivery.
erro_alternative2:
\$24,000 and \$25,000 incorrectly assumes the seller receives the
maximum fee regardless of actual savings calculations, and incorrectly
assigns the full cost savings to the buyer without applying the sharing
ratio.
erro_alternative3:
\$15,000 and \$25,000 incorrectly gives the seller only the
predetermined fee without adding their share of the cost savings
incentive, defeating the purpose of the incentive fee structure.
erro_alternative4:
\$18,750 and \$21,250 reverses the amounts between seller and buyer,
incorrectly giving the seller the buyer\'s 75% share and the buyer the
seller\'s 25% share.

statement:
As a project manager, you have managed several projects in your
organization. You notice that not all the projects are given the proper
support and importance from higher management. You also notice that
projects get terminated while in progress due to other higher-priority
projects in the organization. You will be managing an IT project soon
and want to make sure that your project will get the required support
from the performing organization. What will be your best course of action?
option1:
Request a formal meeting with senior management to secure their
commitment before starting the project
option2:
Develop a comprehensive business case with detailed cost-benefit
analysis to justify the project investment
option3:
Correlate the need for the project to the organizational strategic
objective and goal
option4:
Establish clear success metrics and key performance indicators to
demonstrate project value
explanation:
Projects that align with organizational strategic objectives and goals
receive higher priority, better support, and more consistent resource
allocation from senior management because they directly contribute to
achieving the organization\'s mission and strategic direction. By
correlating the IT project\'s need to strategic objectives, the project
manager demonstrates the project\'s value in advancing organizational
priorities, making it less likely to be terminated or deprioritized
compared to projects with weak strategic alignment. Organizations use
strategic objectives to make portfolio decisions about which projects to
fund, continue, or terminate, so projects with clear strategic alignment
naturally receive protection during resource constraints or competing
priorities. This correlation should be documented in the business case
and project charter, establishing the strategic context that will
sustain support throughout the project lifecycle regardless of changing
circumstances or new project proposals.
erro_alternative1:
Requesting formal meetings with senior management may secure temporary
commitment but does not address the underlying issue of strategic
alignment that determines sustained support and priority when competing
projects emerge.
erro_alternative2:
Developing a comprehensive business case with cost-benefit analysis is
valuable but insufficient without demonstrating strategic alignment, as
financially sound projects still get terminated if they do not advance
organizational strategic objectives.
erro_alternative4:
Establishing clear success metrics and key performance indicators helps
measure project performance but does not ensure organizational support,
as projects with excellent metrics still get terminated if they lack
strategic alignment to organizational goals.

statement:
While managing an ERP project, you realize the targeted project end date
will be delayed by several days. Upon further investigation, you
identify some activities that can be performed in parallel. You also
realize that you have not fully utilized some of your resources in the
project. What will be the BEST course of action in this situation?
option1:
Request a deadline extension from the sponsor and update stakeholders on
the revised timeline
option2:
Update the schedule baseline to reflect the new end date and document
lessons learned
option3: Apply fast tracking and crashing
option4:
Perform Monte Carlo simulation to determine the probability of meeting
the original deadline
explanation:
Fast tracking and crashing are schedule compression techniques used to
bring delayed projects back on track when possible. Fast tracking
involves performing activities in parallel that were originally planned
in sequence, which directly addresses the project manager\'s finding
that some activities can be performed in parallel. Crashing involves
adding resources to critical path activities to reduce their duration,
which directly addresses the identification that some resources have not
been fully utilized in the project. By applying both techniques
together, the project manager uses the available options to compress the
schedule and potentially meet the original deadline without requesting
extensions or changing the baseline. Fast tracking introduces some
schedule risk due to potential rework from parallel activities, while
crashing may increase costs due to additional resource allocation, but
both techniques leverage opportunities already identified in the project
to recover schedule performance before resorting to baseline changes or
deadline extensions.
erro_alternative1:
Requesting a deadline extension from the sponsor should only be
considered after attempting schedule compression techniques, as the
project manager has identified viable opportunities for fast tracking
and crashing that may eliminate the delay.
erro_alternative2:
Updating the schedule baseline to reflect the new end date accepts the
delay without attempting available schedule compression techniques that
could bring the project back on track to meet the original commitment.
erro_alternative4:
Performing Monte Carlo simulation provides probabilistic analysis but
does not take action to address the delay, and the project manager has
already identified concrete opportunities for schedule compression that
should be implemented rather than analyzed further.

statement:
A project manager is preparing for a major procurement to select a
vendor for a software development project. The procurement team has
prepared the Request for Proposal (RFP) and is now planning to hold a
bidding conference with all potential sellers who received the RFP. Which one of the following is NOT true about the goal and objective of a
bidding conference?
option1:
Ensure all potential sellers have a clear and equal understanding of the
procurement requirements
option2:
Provide an opportunity for sellers to ask questions and seek
clarification on the RFP
option3:
Address any ambiguities or uncertainties in the procurement documents
before proposals are submitted
option4:
Select the seller who is best capable of offering the lowest cost for
the project work
explanation:
A bidding conference (also called a bidder conference or pre-bid
conference) is held before proposals are submitted to ensure all
potential sellers have equal access to information, can ask questions,
and receive clarifications about the procurement requirements and
evaluation criteria. The purpose is to promote fair competition by
ensuring no seller has an information advantage over others and that all
sellers understand the requirements identically. Seller selection occurs
later during the proposal evaluation process after all proposals have
been received and evaluated against predetermined criteria, which may
include cost but also factors like technical capability, experience,
schedule, and risk. The bidding conference specifically does not involve
selecting sellers or evaluating proposals, as this would violate
procurement fairness principles by making selection decisions before all
sellers have submitted their proposals based on complete and equal
information.
erro_alternative1:
Ensuring all potential sellers have clear and equal understanding of
procurement requirements is a primary goal of bidding conferences,
promoting fair competition by eliminating information asymmetry between
sellers.
erro_alternative2:
Providing opportunity for sellers to ask questions and seek
clarification on the RFP is a core objective of bidding conferences,
allowing sellers to understand requirements fully before investing time
and resources in proposal development.
erro_alternative3:
Addressing ambiguities or uncertainties in procurement documents before
proposals are submitted prevents misunderstandings, reduces the
likelihood of proposals that do not meet requirements, and ensures all
sellers are responding to the same clearly understood requirements.

statement:
In a negotiated Cost Plus Incentive Fee (CPIF) contract, a project
manager is working with a vendor on a software implementation project.
The contract includes the following terms: a target cost of \$355,000, a
target fee of \$45,000, and a sharing ratio of 75/25 (buyer/seller). The
project has been completed, and the actual cost of the project is
\$390,000. What will be the final fee to the seller?
explanation:
In a CPIF contract, when actual costs exceed the target cost (cost
overrun), the seller\'s fee is reduced by their share of the overrun
according to the predetermined sharing ratio. First, calculate the cost
overrun: \$390,000 (actual) - \$355,000 (target) = \$35,000 overrun.
Next, apply the 75/25 sharing ratio to determine how much of the overrun
reduces the seller\'s fee: the seller bears 25% of the overrun (\$35,000
× 0.25 = \$8,750). The seller\'s final fee equals the target fee minus
their share of the cost overrun: \$45,000 - \$8,750 = \$36,250. This
mechanism incentivizes the seller to control costs by reducing their
profit when costs exceed targets, while the buyer absorbs 75% of the
overrun, balancing risk between both parties and encouraging
cost-conscious project delivery without completely penalizing the seller
for cost increases that may result from unforeseen circumstances or
necessary changes.
erro_alternative2:
\$45,000 incorrectly assumes the seller receives the full target fee
despite the cost overrun, ignoring the incentive fee adjustment
mechanism that is the defining characteristic of CPIF contracts.
erro_alternative3:
\$53,750 incorrectly adds the seller\'s share of the overrun to the
target fee (\$45,000 + \$8,750) as if this were a cost savings rather
than a cost overrun, reversing the financial consequence.
erro_alternative4:
\$27,500 incorrectly subtracts the buyer\'s 75% share of the overrun
from the target fee (\$45,000 - \$26,250), applying the wrong percentage
of the sharing ratio to the fee adjustment calculation.

statement:
A project manager is initiating a new product development project and is
meeting with the sponsor to discuss the project\'s foundation. The
sponsor emphasizes that the product must be launched before the
competitor\'s similar product hits the market in six months, the budget
cannot exceed \$500,000 due to board approval limits, and the product
must meet specific regulatory quality standards. The project manager
needs to document these limitations that will shape project planning and
execution decisions. What should the project manager document these limitations as in the
project charter?
option1: Project assumptions
option2: Project risks
option3: Project requirements
option4: Project constraints
explanation:
Project constraints are limiting factors that restrict or dictate the
project manager\'s options during planning and execution, typically
including scope, schedule, budget, quality, resources, and risk. The
sponsor has identified three clear constraints: a fixed deadline (six
months to launch before competitor), a budget limit (\$500,000 maximum),
and mandatory quality standards (regulatory requirements). These are
constraints rather than assumptions because they represent firm
boundaries that cannot be negotiated or changed without sponsor
approval, and they will directly limit the project manager\'s decisions
about scope, resource allocation, and delivery approaches. Constraints
must be documented in the project charter to establish the boundaries
within which the project team must operate, ensuring all stakeholders
understand the limitations that will shape project planning, and
providing the foundation for managing competing demands among scope,
schedule, cost, quality, resources, and risk throughout the project
lifecycle.
erro_alternative1:
Project assumptions are factors considered true, real, or certain
without proof or demonstration for planning purposes, but the sponsor\'s
limitations are confirmed restrictions rather than uncertain
suppositions that require validation.
erro_alternative2:
Project risks are uncertain events or conditions that, if they occur,
have positive or negative effects on project objectives, but the
sponsor\'s limitations are certain constraints that definitely exist
rather than potential future events.
erro_alternative3:
Project requirements define what the project must deliver or achieve to
satisfy stakeholder needs, while constraints define the boundaries and
limitations within which those requirements must be delivered, making
constraints the more accurate categorization for budget limits,
deadlines, and mandatory standards.

statement:
You have been assigned to identify the key stakeholders for an internal
project that has recently been formally approved. The project involves
implementing a new enterprise-wide knowledge management system. You need
to begin the stakeholder identification process to ensure all relevant
individuals and groups are engaged appropriately throughout the project. Which of the following is your starting point?
option1: Project Charter
option2: Stakeholder Register
option3: Organizational Process Assets
option4: Communications Management Plan
explanation:
The Project Charter is the starting point for stakeholder identification
because it is created during project initiation and formally authorizes
the project\'s existence, providing the initial high-level information
needed to begin identifying stakeholders. The charter identifies the
project sponsor, describes the project purpose and objectives, lists
initial known requirements, defines high-level scope boundaries, and may
identify some key stakeholders or stakeholder groups who authorized or
will be significantly affected by the project. Since the project has
recently been formally approved, the Project Charter exists and contains
the foundational information necessary to begin systematic stakeholder
identification. The charter provides context about what the project will
deliver, why it exists, and who has authority over it, all of which are
essential for determining who should be identified as stakeholders. From
this starting point, the project manager can then develop more
comprehensive stakeholder identification using additional inputs like
organizational charts, lessons learned, and expert judgment.
erro_alternative2:
Stakeholder Register is an output of the stakeholder identification
process, not an input or starting point, as it documents identified
stakeholders and their information after identification activities have
been completed.
erro_alternative3:
Organizational Process Assets are valuable inputs for stakeholder
identification and contain historical information and lessons learned
from previous projects, but the Project Charter provides the specific
project context necessary to begin identification for this particular
project.
erro_alternative4:
Communications Management Plan is developed later during project
planning after stakeholders have been identified, as it defines how
communications will be managed based on stakeholder communication
requirements that can only be determined after stakeholders are known.

statement:
A project manager is reviewing the activities their team performs
throughout project execution. The team tracks earned value metrics every
week, reviews variance reports, updates the risk register when new risks
emerge, evaluates change requests through the change control board, and
adjusts resource allocations when performance deviates from the plan. What Focus Area do these activities represent?
option1: Planning
option2: Monitoring and Controlling
option3: Executing
option4: Closing
explanation:
The Monitoring and Controlling Focus Area encompasses activities that
track project performance, compare actual results against baselines,
identify variances, evaluate changes, and implement corrective or
preventive actions. Tracking earned value, reviewing variance reports,
updating risk registers with new information, evaluating change
requests, and adjusting resources based on performance all represent
monitoring and controlling work. These activities run concurrently with
execution throughout the project to ensure the project remains aligned
with objectives and baselines.
erro_alternative1:
Planning involves developing the initial approach, baselines, and
detailed plans before execution begins, not the ongoing performance
tracking and variance analysis described.
erro_alternative3:
Executing involves performing the work to create deliverables and
implement plans, not measuring performance against baselines or
evaluating whether corrective actions are needed.
erro_alternative4:
Closing involves finalizing all activities, obtaining acceptance, and
releasing resources at project end, not the ongoing performance
monitoring that occurs throughout execution.

statement:
Your team has estimated that there is a 40 percent probability of a
delay in the receipt of required parts with an additional cost of
\$15,000 to the project. This delay will also cost the company \$20,000
in lost business. Upon further investigation, you identified that if an
existing component could be adapted it would save the project \$10,500
in engineering costs. There is a 30 percent probability that the team
can take advantage of that opportunity. What is the total Expected Monetary Value (EMV) of these two events?
explanation:
Expected Monetary Value (EMV) is calculated by multiplying the
probability of each event by its monetary impact, then summing all EMV
values to determine the overall expected value of multiple uncertain
events. For the parts delay risk: the probability is 40% and the total
negative impact includes both the \$15,000 additional project cost and
the \$20,000 in lost business, totaling \$35,000 in costs, so EMV = 0.40
× (-\$35,000) = -\$14,000. For the component adaptation opportunity: the
probability is 30% and the positive impact is \$10,500 in engineering
cost savings, so EMV = 0.30 × \$10,500 = \$3,150. The total EMV combines
both events: -\$14,000 + \$3,150 = -\$10,850. The negative total EMV
indicates that the expected monetary impact of these combined uncertain
events represents a net cost to the project, with the threat\'s expected
impact exceeding the opportunity\'s expected benefit, which should be
factored into contingency reserve planning and risk response strategy
development.
erro_alternative1:
\$3,150 represents only the EMV of the opportunity (adapting the
existing component) while ignoring the EMV of the risk (parts delay),
failing to calculate the total expected monetary value of both events
combined.
erro_alternative3:
--\$14,000 represents only the EMV of the risk (parts delay) while
ignoring the EMV of the opportunity (component adaptation), providing an
incomplete picture of the combined expected monetary impact.
erro_alternative4:
--\$24,500 incorrectly subtracts the opportunity\'s full impact rather
than its EMV from the risk\'s EMV (-\$14,000 - \$10,500 = -\$24,500),
failing to apply probability to the opportunity and treating the savings
as certain rather than having only 30% likelihood.

statement:
Who should determine the work that should be placed in the iteration
backlog?
option1: Product Owner
option2: Scrum Master
option3: Development Team
option4: Project Sponsor
explanation:
The Development Team is responsible for determining which work from the
product backlog should be placed in the iteration backlog (also called
sprint backlog) because the team members are best positioned to assess
their own capacity, understand the technical complexity of the work, and
make realistic commitments about what they can complete during the
iteration. During sprint planning, the Product Owner presents
prioritized product backlog items and explains the sprint goal, but the
Development Team has the autonomy to select which items they can commit
to delivering based on their velocity, available capacity, technical
dependencies, and understanding of the work required. This
self-organization principle is fundamental to agile methodologies
because it empowers the team to make sustainable commitments, promotes
team accountability for sprint results, and respects the team\'s
technical expertise in estimating their own work capacity rather than
having work assignments imposed by others who may not understand the
technical complexity or team capabilities.
erro_alternative1:
Product Owner is responsible for prioritizing the product backlog and
defining the sprint goal but does not determine which specific items the
team commits to in the iteration backlog, as this would violate the
principle of team self-organization and could lead to unrealistic
commitments.
erro_alternative2:
Scrum Master facilitates the sprint planning process and helps remove
impediments but does not determine what work goes into the iteration
backlog, as the Scrum Master serves the team rather than directing their
work commitments.
erro_alternative4:
Project Sponsor provides project funding and high-level direction but is
not involved in iteration-level planning decisions about which specific
work items the team commits to completing, as this is an operational
team decision rather than a strategic oversight function.

statement: Which of the following is TRUE about stakeholders?
option1:
Stakeholder identification should be completed during project initiation
and documented in the project charter
option2:
Stakeholder identification is a continuous and sometimes strenuous
process
option3:
Once stakeholders are identified and registered, their influence and
impact levels remain constant throughout the project
option4:
The project sponsor and key executives are the only stakeholders who
require ongoing engagement throughout the project lifecycle
explanation:
Stakeholder identification is an iterative process that continues
throughout the project lifecycle because stakeholders can emerge, change
roles, or become relevant at different project phases, making it
impossible to identify all stakeholders completely during project
initiation. New stakeholders may appear due to organizational changes,
regulatory requirements, project scope changes, or increased project
visibility as deliverables materialize and impact becomes clearer to
previously unaware groups. The process can be strenuous because it
requires continuous vigilance, relationship building, political
awareness, and effort to uncover stakeholders who may not be immediately
obvious, including those who prefer to remain unidentified or who emerge
only when specific project phases or deliverables affect their
interests. Long-term projects like the three-year infrastructure project
mentioned are particularly susceptible to stakeholder changes due to
organizational restructuring, personnel turnover, policy changes, and
evolving community concerns that introduce new stakeholders as the
project progresses through different phases and geographic areas.
erro_alternative1:
Stakeholder identification cannot be completed only during project
initiation because new stakeholders continuously emerge throughout the
project lifecycle, and limiting identification to initiation would miss
critical stakeholders who become relevant during later project phases.
erro_alternative3:
Stakeholder influence and impact levels change throughout the project as
deliverables are produced, risks materialize, organizational priorities
shift, and stakeholders\' roles or interests evolve, requiring
continuous reassessment rather than assuming static stakeholder
attributes.
erro_alternative4:
Project sponsors and key executives are important stakeholders but
represent only a subset of all stakeholders requiring ongoing
engagement, as projects typically involve numerous stakeholders
including team members, customers, end users, suppliers, regulatory
bodies, community groups, and others who need appropriate engagement
throughout the project lifecycle.

statement:
Which process is MOST closely associated with continuous process
improvement?
option1: Control Quality
option2: Manage Quality
option3: Plan Quality Management
option4: Perform Integrated Change Control
explanation:
Manage Quality is the process of translating the quality management plan
into executable quality activities that incorporate the organization\'s
quality policies into the project, and it includes continuous process
improvement as a core activity. This process focuses on improving
processes to prevent defects rather than just detecting them, using
quality audits to identify inefficient or ineffective processes, and
implementing process improvements based on quality measurements and
analysis. Continuous process improvement approaches like
Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA), Six Sigma, and Total Quality Management (TQM)
are applied during Manage Quality to systematically enhance process
performance, reduce waste, eliminate non-value-added activities, and
increase efficiency and effectiveness. By focusing on process
improvement rather than just deliverable inspection, Manage Quality
addresses root causes of quality issues, creates sustainable
improvements that benefit the current project and future projects, and
shifts the quality paradigm from detection to prevention through
systematic process enhancement.
erro_alternative1:
Control Quality focuses on monitoring and recording quality activity
results to assess performance and recommend necessary changes,
emphasizing inspection and measurement of deliverables rather than
improving the processes that create those deliverables.
erro_alternative3:
Plan Quality Management establishes quality requirements, objectives,
and standards for the project and deliverables, and documents how the
project will demonstrate compliance, but it focuses on planning rather
than the ongoing execution of process improvements throughout the
project.
erro_alternative4:
Perform Integrated Change Control reviews all change requests, approves
changes, and manages changes to deliverables, baselines, and project
documents, but it manages changes generally rather than specifically
driving continuous quality process improvements.

statement:
You are trying to identify and categorize as many potential risks as
possible in the mission critical project that you are overseeing. You
want to ensure comprehensive risk identification by using all available
resources and techniques to uncover threats and opportunities that could
affect project objectives. Which of the following will NOT help you with your effort of identifying
risks?
option1: Delphi Technique
option2: Brainstorming
option3: SWOT Analysis
option4: Risk Register
explanation:
The Risk Register is an output of the Identify Risks process, not an
input or tool that helps with identifying risks initially. The Risk
Register documents identified risks, their characteristics, and
potential responses after risks have been identified through various
identification techniques and inputs. You cannot use the Risk Register
to help identify risks during initial risk identification because it
does not exist until the Identify Risks process creates it by
documenting the results of risk identification activities. While the
Risk Register may be reviewed and updated iteratively as new risks are
discovered throughout the project lifecycle, it is the result of risk
identification efforts rather than a resource that facilitates the
initial identification of risks. During the Identify Risks process,
project managers use inputs like project documents, organizational
process assets, enterprise environmental factors, and the risk
management plan along with techniques like brainstorming, interviews,
and expert judgment to create the Risk Register as the primary output.
erro_alternative1:
Delphi Technique is a risk identification method that uses anonymous
expert input gathered through multiple rounds of questionnaires to
achieve consensus on potential risks, eliminating bias from dominant
personalities and allowing experts to identify risks independently
before converging on comprehensive risk lists.
erro_alternative2:
Brainstorming is a widely used risk identification technique where
project team members, subject matter experts, and stakeholders generate
ideas about potential risks in facilitated group sessions, leveraging
diverse perspectives and group dynamics to identify risks that
individuals might miss working alone.
erro_alternative3:
SWOT Analysis (Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, Threats) is a
structured risk identification technique that examines internal project
strengths and weaknesses alongside external opportunities and threats,
helping identify risks by analyzing how weaknesses could lead to threats
and how strengths could be leveraged to capitalize on opportunities.

statement:
A project manager is leading a software development project using Scrum
methodology. The team is currently in the middle of a two-week Sprint
and has committed to delivering specific user stories. Various
stakeholders and team members are suggesting different changes to the
Sprint. The Scrum Master needs to determine which changes would violate
Scrum principles and should be prevented. While a Sprint is currently being done which of the following should not
be changed?
option1: Product backlog content
option2: Product backlog prioritization
option3: Team member composition
option4: Stakeholder communication methods
explanation:
Team composition should remain stable during a Sprint to maintain team
focus, preserve established working relationships, protect team velocity
and productivity, and honor the commitment the team made during Sprint
Planning based on their known capacity and capabilities. Changing team
members mid-Sprint disrupts team dynamics, requires time for new members
to understand context and get up to speed, may invalidate velocity-based
commitments made during planning, and violates the Scrum principle of
maintaining stable, self-organizing teams that can focus on delivering
the Sprint Goal without external disruption. While emergencies
occasionally necessitate team changes, these should be exceptional
circumstances rather than normal practice, and the Scrum Master should
protect the team from mid-Sprint composition changes that would
undermine their ability to deliver on commitments. Stable team
composition across Sprints allows teams to develop working rhythms,
improve collaboration effectiveness, build shared understanding, and
continuously improve velocity and predictability over time.
erro_alternative1:
Product backlog content can be modified at any time during the Sprint as
new requirements emerge, stakeholders identify additional needs, or
market conditions change, because the product backlog is a living
artifact that continuously evolves independently of Sprint execution.
erro_alternative2:
Product backlog prioritization is the Product Owner\'s responsibility
and can be adjusted during the Sprint as business priorities shift,
market conditions change, or stakeholder needs evolve, though these
changes do not affect the current Sprint\'s committed work unless the
Sprint Goal becomes obsolete.
erro_alternative4:
Stakeholder communication methods can be adjusted during the Sprint if
current approaches prove ineffective, stakeholder needs change, or
better communication channels are identified, as effective stakeholder
engagement requires flexibility and responsiveness to evolving
communication needs.

statement:
Which of the following statements is FALSE about qualified sellers in a
project?
option1:
Qualified sellers can be identified from organizational process assets
based on past performance
option2:
A list of qualified sellers helps determine which sellers receive
solicitation documents
option3:
Qualified sellers are those who have been pre-screened and meet specific
qualification criteria
option4: It is a tool in the Conduct Procurement process
explanation:
Qualified sellers list is an input to the Conduct Procurement process,
not a tool used during the process. Tools are methods, techniques, or
instruments used to perform activities and produce outputs, while inputs
are the information, documents, or resources that are used to perform
the process. The qualified sellers list typically comes from
organizational process assets or from a separate qualification process
and serves as an input that informs which sellers should receive
solicitation documents and be invited to submit proposals. This list
contains sellers who have been pre-qualified through past performance
evaluation, capability assessments, financial stability reviews, or
other qualification criteria established by the organization. By using
the qualified sellers list as an input, project managers reduce
procurement risk by limiting solicitations to sellers with proven
capability, save time and resources by avoiding evaluation of
unqualified sellers, and increase the likelihood of successful
procurement outcomes by engaging only sellers who meet minimum
qualification standards.

statement:
The Change Control Board (CCB) has just approved a change request to
modify one of the major deliverables in a manufacturing project. The
project manager now needs to ensure that the approved change is properly
executed. The team is waiting for direction on how to proceed with
implementing this modification to the project scope. In which process will this change be implemented?
option1: Perform Integrated Change Control
option2: Monitor and Control Project Work
option3: Validate Scope
option4: Direct and Manage Project Work
explanation:
Direct and Manage Project Work is the process where approved change
requests are implemented. This process involves executing the work
defined in the project management plan to achieve project objectives,
and it includes implementing approved changes to deliverables,
processes, baselines, or project documents. Once the CCB approves a
change request through the Perform Integrated Change Control process,
the approved change becomes an input to Direct and Manage Project Work,
where the project manager and team execute the change by modifying
deliverables, adjusting work activities, reallocating resources, or
taking other actions necessary to implement the approved change. This
process transforms approved change requests into actual modifications to
project deliverables and work, ensures changes are coordinated with
other project activities, and updates project documents and work
performance data to reflect the implementation of approved changes
throughout project execution.
erro_alternative1:
Perform Integrated Change Control is the process where change requests
are reviewed, evaluated, and approved or rejected by the CCB, but it
does not implement the changes; it only authorizes them for
implementation in other processes.
erro_alternative2:
Monitor and Control Project Work tracks, reviews, and reports project
progress against the performance baseline and identifies variances that
may require change requests, but it does not implement approved changes;
it monitors performance and identifies the need for changes.
erro_alternative3:
Validate Scope is the process of formally accepting completed
deliverables by obtaining stakeholder acceptance, but it does not
implement changes to deliverables; it verifies that deliverables meet
acceptance criteria and obtains formal acceptance from customers or
sponsors.

statement:
You have been assigned as a project manager for a project expected to
last five months. The project has a budget of \$450,000 and should be
implemented in three different departments in your organization. While
reviewing the status of the project after two months, you find that the
project is 35 percent complete. Which of the following statements is TRUE about your project?
option1:
A. Your project is on schedule because work is progressing across all
three departments B. Your project is behind schedule C. Your project is
ahead of schedule because more than one-third of the work is complete D.
Insufficient information exists to determine schedule performance
without actual cost data
option2:
Your project is on schedule because work is progressing across all three
departments
option3: Your project is behind schedule
option4:
Your project is ahead of schedule because more than one-third of the
work is complete
option5:
Insufficient information exists to determine schedule performance
without actual cost data
explanation:
The project is behind schedule because after two months of a five-month
project, 40 percent of the planned duration has elapsed (2 months ÷ 5
months = 0.40) but only 35 percent of the work has been completed. This
indicates that work is not progressing at the planned rate and the
project is falling behind the schedule baseline. The Schedule
Performance Index (SPI) can be calculated by comparing earned value to
planned value: if we assume the project was planned for linear progress,
the planned value at two months would be 40 percent while the earned
value (actual work completed) is only 35 percent, yielding an SPI of
0.875 (35% ÷ 40% = 0.875). Since the SPI is less than 1.0, this confirms
the project is behind schedule and if this trend continues without
corrective action, the project will not be completed within the planned
five-month duration. The project manager should analyze the schedule
variance, identify root causes for the delay, and implement corrective
actions to bring the project back on track.

statement:
Your organization has enough resources only to work on one of two
potential projects and decides to use the Net Present Value (NPV) to
assess and select the best project for the organization. Project Alpha
has an NPV of \$45,000 and Project Beta has an NPV of \$60,000. Project
Alpha will take 3 years to complete and Project Beta will take 4 years. What will be the opportunity cost of the project chosen?
explanation:
Opportunity cost represents the value of the next best alternative that
is foregone when making a decision between mutually exclusive options.
Since Project Beta has a higher NPV (\$60,000) compared to Project Alpha
(\$45,000), the organization will select Project Beta for execution
based on maximizing net present value. By choosing Project Beta, the
organization gives up the opportunity to realize the \$45,000 NPV from
Project Alpha, making \$45,000 the opportunity cost of the decision.
Opportunity cost is always measured as the benefit of the option not
selected, not the benefit of the option that is selected. Understanding
opportunity cost is critical for portfolio and project selection
decisions because it quantifies what the organization sacrifices by
choosing one project over another, allowing decision makers to evaluate
whether the incremental benefit of the selected project justifies
forgoing alternative investments.
erro_alternative1:
\$60,000 represents the NPV of Project Beta, which is the benefit gained
from the selected project rather than the opportunity cost, as
opportunity cost measures what is given up, not what is gained from the
chosen alternative.
erro_alternative3:
\$15,000 represents the difference between the two projects\' NPVs
(\$60,000 - \$45,000), which shows the incremental benefit of choosing
Project Beta over Project Alpha but does not represent the opportunity
cost of forgoing Project Alpha entirely.
erro_alternative4:
\$105,000 represents the sum of both projects\' NPVs (\$60,000 +
\$45,000), which is not meaningful in opportunity cost analysis because
the organization can only select one project due to resource
constraints, making the combined value irrelevant to the decision.

statement:
A project manager has completed the procurement planning and developed
bid documents for specialized equipment needed for a manufacturing
project. The project manager now needs to obtain proposals from
qualified vendors, evaluate their submissions against established
criteria, negotiate terms and conditions, and award the contract to the
selected supplier. What process is the project manager performing?
option1: Plan Sourcing Strategy
option2: Conduct Procurements
option3: Control Procurements
option4: Close Procurements
explanation:
Conduct Procurements is the process of obtaining seller responses,
selecting a seller, and awarding a contract. This includes advertising
the procurement opportunity, conducting bidder conferences, receiving
and evaluating proposals or bids, selecting the best-qualified seller
based on evaluation criteria, negotiating contract terms, and executing
the procurement agreement. The activities described (obtaining
proposals, evaluating submissions, negotiating, and awarding contracts)
represent the core activities of the Conduct Procurements process.
erro_alternative1:
Plan Sourcing Strategy determines whether to make or buy and develops
the overall procurement approach, which has already been completed
before soliciting vendor proposals.
erro_alternative3:
Control Procurements manages ongoing procurement relationships, monitors
vendor performance, and administers contract changes after the contract
has been awarded and work is underway.
erro_alternative4:
Close Procurements finalizes all procurement activities, obtains formal
acceptance, and closes contracts at the completion of procurement work
or project end.

statement:
To have an effective daily standup meeting which of the following should
be avoided?
option1: Team members standing during the meeting
option2: Keeping the meeting timeboxed to 15 minutes
option3: Team members synchronizing on sprint progress
option4: Team members discussing personal issues
explanation:
Daily standup meetings should remain focused on project work, sprint
progress, and team coordination rather than personal matters unrelated
to work. The standup is a brief synchronization meeting where team
members share what they accomplished yesterday, what they plan to do
today, and any impediments blocking their progress. Discussing personal
issues during the standup wastes the entire team\'s time, distracts from
project coordination objectives, and violates the timebox constraint
that keeps standups efficient and focused. While team cohesion and
personal relationships are important for team performance, personal
discussions should occur outside the structured standup meeting in
informal conversations, team-building activities, or one-on-one
interactions. The standup\'s purpose is work coordination and impediment
identification, not social interaction or personal problem-solving,
making personal issue discussions inappropriate for this specific
meeting format.
erro_alternative1:
Team members standing during the meeting is an intentional practice that
encourages brevity and focus by creating mild physical discomfort that
discourages lengthy discussions, though some teams successfully conduct
effective standups while seated if the timebox and focus are maintained.
erro_alternative2:
Keeping the meeting timeboxed to 15 minutes is a best practice that
ensures the standup remains efficient and focused, preventing the
meeting from expanding into lengthy status reports or problem-solving
sessions that should occur separately.
erro_alternative3:
Team members synchronizing on sprint progress is a primary objective of
the daily standup, allowing the team to coordinate work, identify
dependencies, adjust plans based on current status, and ensure everyone
understands how the sprint is progressing toward the sprint goal.

statement:
You are a project manager working for the healthcare system and have
been tasked with overseeing the implementation of a new network
infrastructure at the local hospital. A month into the project, you just
finished creating the Project Charter and identifying the key
stakeholders. You are about to move to the planning process group when
the hospital authority requests that you provide them with the budget
and cost baseline.
option1: Your best response should be:
option2:
Provide the high-level budget estimate documented in the Project Charter
as the cost baseline
option3:
Request additional time to gather historical data from similar network
infrastructure projects
option4:
Inform them that the budget and cost baseline will be finalized during
the planning process
option5:
Escalate the request to the project sponsor for budget approval
authority
explanation:
The cost baseline is developed during the planning process group,
specifically as an output of the Determine Budget process, and cannot be
provided during project initiation when only the Project Charter and
stakeholder identification have been completed. The Project Charter
contains high-level budget information or funding requirements, but this
is not the same as the detailed cost baseline that aggregates estimated
costs of individual activities or work packages and establishes the
time-phased budget against which project cost performance will be
measured. To create the cost baseline, the project manager must first
complete planning processes including scope definition, WBS creation,
activity definition, activity sequencing, resource estimation, activity
duration estimation, and cost estimation before aggregating these
detailed estimates into a time-phased cost baseline. By informing
stakeholders that the budget and cost baseline will be finalized during
planning, the project manager sets appropriate expectations about when
detailed cost information will be available and maintains process
integrity by completing planning activities in the proper sequence.
erro_alternative1:
Providing the high-level budget estimate from the Project Charter as the
cost baseline misrepresents preliminary funding information as detailed
cost baseline data, potentially leading to unrealistic expectations and
improper cost control since the charter\'s rough estimates lack the
detail and time-phasing required for effective cost management.
erro_alternative2:
Requesting additional time to gather historical data from similar
projects does not address the fundamental issue that detailed planning
activities must be completed before the cost baseline can be developed,
and implies the project manager could provide the baseline sooner with
more data rather than explaining the necessary planning process
sequence.
erro_alternative3:
Escalating the request to the project sponsor for budget approval
authority misunderstands the request, as the hospital authority is
asking for the cost baseline itself rather than approval authority, and
escalation does not resolve the fact that the baseline cannot exist
until planning processes are completed.

statement:
Higher management has assigned you as the project manager very early in
the project life cycle for a very critical project. You were asked to
start working on the Project Charter immediately.
option1: You will be performing all of the following activities EXCEPT:
option2: Develop the project Scope Statement Document
option3: Document high-level requirements
option4: Identify key stakeholders
option5: Document assumptions and constraints
explanation:
The Project Scope Statement is developed during the planning process
group as an output of the Define Scope process, not during project
initiation when the Project Charter is being created. The Project
Charter contains high-level scope information such as project
objectives, high-level requirements, and major deliverables, but it does
not include the detailed scope statement that defines project and
product scope in detail, describes major deliverables, defines product
acceptance criteria, and identifies what is excluded from the project.
The detailed Project Scope Statement requires completion of activities
like stakeholder requirement gathering, scope decomposition, and
detailed analysis that occur after the project is formally authorized
through the approved Project Charter. Attempting to develop the detailed
scope statement before the project is formally authorized through the
charter would be premature and could waste effort on planning activities
before the project has official sanction to proceed.
erro_alternative1:
Documenting high-level requirements is an essential activity during
Project Charter development, as the charter must identify key
requirements and characteristics that will influence the project
approach, even though detailed requirements analysis occurs later during
planning.
erro_alternative3:
Identifying key stakeholders is performed during Project Charter
development to ensure major stakeholders are recognized early and can
provide input to charter contents, though comprehensive stakeholder
identification continues throughout the project as an iterative process.
erro_alternative4:
Documenting assumptions and constraints is a critical activity during
Project Charter development, as the charter must capture initial
assumptions about resources, schedules, budgets, and external factors,
plus constraints that will limit project options and approaches.

statement:
You are a shy project manager struggling with your communication skills.
You like to keep to yourself unless you are prompted for your input. On
the other hand, Steve is highly regarded by everyone and very well
liked. You consider Steve to be your role model as he is capable of
getting others to see his way on many issues without relying on formal
authority or position. What type of power does Steve possess?
option1: Expert power
option2: Legitimate power
option3: Referent power
option4: Reward power
explanation:
Referent power is based on personal characteristics, respect,
admiration, and the strength of relationships that make others want to
follow or agree with someone. Steve possesses referent power because he
is highly regarded, well-liked, and capable of influencing others to see
his perspective based on his personal appeal and the respect he has
earned rather than formal authority, expertise, or ability to provide
rewards or punishments. People with referent power influence others
through charisma, interpersonal skills, and the positive relationships
they build, making others want to align with their views because they
admire and trust them. This type of power is particularly valuable in
project management because it enables influence without formal authority
over team members, creates voluntary cooperation rather than compliance
through coercion, and builds strong team cohesion based on mutual
respect and positive working relationships that persist beyond
individual projects.
erro_alternative1:
Expert power is based on specialized knowledge, skills, or expertise
that others recognize and respect, but Steve\'s influence comes from
being well-liked and highly regarded as a person rather than from
technical or specialized knowledge that makes him an authority in a
particular domain.
erro_alternative2:
Legitimate power derives from formal position or authority within the
organizational hierarchy, but Steve\'s ability to influence others does
not rely on formal authority or organizational position, as he
influences through personal characteristics and relationships rather
than positional power.
erro_alternative4:
Reward power is based on the ability to provide or withhold rewards such
as bonuses, promotions, recognition, or preferred assignments, but
Steve\'s influence comes from being well-liked and respected rather than
from controlling rewards that others desire.

statement:
While managing an ERP solution implementation project, you realize that
you are not utilizing your resources evenly in the project. You found
out that you are using almost all of your resources in some of the work
weeks and hardly using any in some other work weeks. You consider moving
around some of the activities and resources so that you can utilize your
resources evenly throughout the project life cycle. Which of the following techniques are you using?
option1: Resource smoothing
option2: Fast tracking
option3: Resource leveling
option4: Crashing
explanation:
Resource leveling is a schedule network analysis technique used to
address resource overallocation, resource conflicts, and uneven resource
utilization by adjusting the start and finish dates of activities to
balance resource demand and achieve more consistent resource usage
throughout the project lifecycle. The project manager\'s approach of
moving activities and resources around to achieve even resource
utilization across work weeks is the defining characteristic of resource
leveling. This technique often extends the project schedule because
activities may need to be delayed to avoid resource peaks and achieve
the desired level resource distribution, but it provides benefits
including reduced resource costs by avoiding expensive overtime or
temporary staff during peak periods, improved resource morale by
avoiding burnout from overwork followed by underutilization, and
simplified resource management by creating predictable resource needs
that are easier to plan and coordinate across multiple projects sharing
the same resource pool.
erro_alternative1:
Resource smoothing is similar to resource leveling but operates under
the constraint that the project completion date cannot change, adjusting
resource assignments within available float to minimize resource
fluctuations without extending the schedule, whereas the scenario
describes resource leveling where schedule extension is acceptable to
achieve even resource usage.
erro_alternative2:
Fast tracking is a schedule compression technique that involves
performing activities in parallel that were originally planned in
sequence to reduce project duration, which does not address resource
utilization evenness and actually could worsen resource peaks by
increasing concurrent activities.
erro_alternative4:
Crashing is a schedule compression technique that involves adding
resources to critical path activities to reduce their duration and
compress the overall schedule, which addresses schedule concerns rather
than resource utilization balance and typically increases resource peaks
rather than leveling them.

statement:
Ashley, a senior project manager, recently took over a project to
produce a safe and effective drug from another project manager who left
the company. Ashley was surprised to find out that there is no Change
Control Board (CCB) to review, approve, or deny change requests for the
project. Upon further investigation, she finds that there is also no
organizational Change Control Board to manage changes for the entire
organization. What should Ashley do in this situation?
option1: Make all change decisions independently without a formal process
option2:
Establish a project-specific Change Management Protocol until the
organization creates an official Change Control Board (CCB)
option3: Wait for the organization to establish a CCB before processing changes
option4: Delegate change approval authority to team leads
explanation:
When no Change Control Board exists at either the project or
organizational level, the project manager should establish a
project-specific change management protocol to ensure proper governance
while the organization develops formal structures. This protocol should
define how change requests are submitted, evaluated, approved, and
implemented, identify who has approval authority for different types of
changes (project manager for minor changes, sponsor for major changes,
etc.), establish evaluation criteria for assessing change impacts, and
document decision-making processes. This interim solution provides
necessary governance to protect project objectives while remaining
flexible enough to adapt when the organization establishes formal CCB
structures. The protocol ensures changes are managed systematically
without waiting indefinitely for organizational governance that may take
time to establish.
erro_alternative1:
Making change decisions independently without formal processes lacks
systematic evaluation, stakeholder input, and documentation that proper
change management requires, even in the absence of a formal CCB.
erro_alternative3:
Waiting for organizational CCB establishment paralyzes the project by
preventing necessary changes from being processed while organizational
structures are developed, which could take months or never occur.
erro_alternative4:
Delegating change approval to team leads distributes authority without
establishing evaluation criteria, processes, or governance ensuring
consistent, informed decisions about changes affecting project
objectives.

statement:
A project manager has collected information about Sabrina, a team
member, from multiple sources including other team members and
management staff. The project manager has identified several areas for
improvement that need to be discussed with Sabrina. The project manager
is particularly concerned that Sabrina is having many conflicts with
other team members. During a one-on-one meeting, both parties agree on a
training program to address the conflicts with other team members. The
project manager also makes sure that Sabrina understands she needs to
submit her status and progress reports at the beginning of every week. Which action is the project manager currently engaged in with Sabrina?
option1: Conflict management
option2: Project performance appraisal
option3: Team development
option4: Stakeholder engagement
explanation:
The project manager is conducting a project performance appraisal (also
called performance review or evaluation), which involves assessing team
member performance by gathering feedback from multiple sources
(360-degree feedback approach), identifying strengths and areas for
improvement, meeting one-on-one to discuss performance issues and
expectations, developing action plans for improvement including
training, and clarifying expectations for future performance (like
report submission). Performance appraisals provide formal feedback to
team members about their contributions, behavior, and development needs.
While the appraisal addresses conflicts and may lead to training, the
overall activity described is a comprehensive performance evaluation
covering multiple performance dimensions including interpersonal
behavior and deliverable responsibilities.
erro_alternative1:
Conflict management addresses specific disagreements or disputes but
doesn\'t encompass the comprehensive performance evaluation, feedback
collection, and development planning described in the scenario.
erro_alternative3:
Team development activities build team capabilities and cohesiveness
broadly but don\'t involve the individual performance assessment,
feedback, and corrective action planning characteristic of performance
appraisals.
erro_alternative4:
Stakeholder engagement involves managing relationships with project
stakeholders but doesn\'t describe the individual team member
performance assessment and development planning occurring in this
one-on-one meeting.

statement:
A project manager is having an issue with one of the manufacturing
processes being used to create the required parts for routers and
switches that the company produces. The project manager needs to
identify the cause of this issue and understand the effect it may have
on the project. What tool should the project manager use to identify the cause of this
issue and its effects?
option1: Pareto chart
option2: Control chart
option3: Ishikawa Diagram
option4: Flowchart
explanation:
An Ishikawa diagram (also called fishbone diagram or cause-and-effect
diagram) is specifically designed to identify causes of problems and
show their relationship to effects. The diagram places the problem
(manufacturing process issue) at the \"head\" of the fish and organizes
potential causes into major categories (the \"bones\") such as methods,
machines, materials, manpower, measurements, and environment. The team
systematically brainstorms potential causes within each category,
helping identify root causes of the manufacturing issue. The
cause-and-effect structure inherent in the Ishikawa diagram\'s design
makes it ideal for understanding both what might be causing the problem
and how those causes relate to the observed effect on manufacturing
quality.
erro_alternative1:
Pareto charts rank problems or defects by frequency to prioritize which
issues to address first but don\'t identify causes of problems or show
cause-effect relationships.
erro_alternative2:
Control charts monitor process performance over time to detect trends or
out-of-control conditions but don\'t help identify what is causing
observed problems or their effects.
erro_alternative4:
Flowcharts map process sequences and workflows to identify bottlenecks
or inefficiencies but don\'t specifically organize and categorize
potential causes to understand cause-and-effect relationships for
problems.

statement:
An Agile team is working on a product with a large backlog containing
over 100 user stories. Many of the stories near the bottom of the
backlog are outdated, poorly defined, or no longer relevant. The product
owner wants to ensure the backlog contains well-defined, prioritized
items that reflect current understanding and business value. What activity should the product owner and team perform to maintain the
backlog?
option1: Build the backlog
option2: Prioritize the backlog
option3: Estimate the backlog
option4: Groom the backlog
explanation:
Grooming the backlog (also called backlog refinement) is the ongoing
activity of reviewing, updating, prioritizing, and refining backlog
items to ensure they are ready for upcoming iterations. Grooming
activities include removing outdated or irrelevant items, breaking large
stories into smaller ones, adding detail and acceptance criteria to
upcoming stories, re-prioritizing based on changing business needs, and
estimating items that haven\'t been sized. The product owner typically
leads grooming sessions with team participation, often dedicating 5-10%
of iteration time to this activity. Regular backlog grooming ensures
upcoming work is well-defined before sprint planning, maintains backlog
quality by removing obsolete items, and keeps priorities aligned with
current business value and understanding.
erro_alternative1:
Building the backlog is an initial activity that creates the product
backlog at project start but doesn\'t address the ongoing maintenance,
refinement, and cleanup needed for large existing backlogs with outdated
items.
erro_alternative2:
Prioritizing the backlog arranges items by importance and value but
doesn\'t encompass removing outdated items, adding details to poorly
defined stories, breaking down large items, or the comprehensive
maintenance grooming provides.
erro_alternative3:
Estimating the backlog sizes stories to enable planning but doesn\'t
include removing obsolete items, refining poorly defined stories, adding
acceptance criteria, or ensuring overall backlog quality beyond just
sizing.

statement:
A project manager is overseeing the development of a new console video
game. The project has 10 components with 5 team members working on it.
There are 15 stakeholders, including the company\'s previous owner. One
component is of major concern because it is difficult to implement. The
project manager found out the soonest work can start on that component
is early next week, and it will take 10 days to complete. The project
manager also found out that as of now CPI = 0.81 and SPI = 1.2. What should be the project manager\'s main concern at this time?
option1: Schedule performance
option2: The difficult component implementation
option3: Stakeholder management with the previous owner
option4: Cost performance
explanation:
The main concern should be cost performance as indicated by CPI = 0.81,
which means the project is getting only 81 cents of value for every
dollar spent, representing significant cost overruns. With SPI = 1.2,
the project is actually ahead of schedule (earning 20% more value than
planned), so schedule is not a concern. While the difficult component
and stakeholder management are considerations, the CPI of 0.81 indicates
serious cost problems requiring immediate attention and corrective
action. Cost overruns of this magnitude (19% over budget based on work
completed) threaten project viability and require the project manager to
investigate causes, implement cost controls, and potentially request
additional funding or reduce scope to stay within budget constraints.
erro_alternative1:
Schedule performance is favorable with SPI = 1.2 showing the project is
ahead of schedule, so schedule should not be the main concern when cost
performance is significantly unfavorable.
erro_alternative2:
While the difficult component requires attention, it\'s a known issue
scheduled for next week, making it a planned concern rather than the
urgent performance problem indicated by poor CPI.
erro_alternative3:
Stakeholder management with the previous owner may require attention but
isn\'t identified as an immediate problem, while the poor cost
performance represents a clear, quantified issue requiring urgent
action.

statement:
While managing a WIFI project, a project manager discovered that a few
team members are not getting along. The project manager also realized
that three team members are not sure how to complete their deliverables.
All team members are working together in the same building. The project
manager has also set up regular weekly meetings with all team members in
a single meeting room. Which technique will NOT be helpful in this situation?
option1: War room
option2: Training
option3: Reward and recognition
option4: Negotiation
explanation:
A war room (also called project room or team room) is NOT helpful in
this situation because the team is already colocated and working
together in the same building with regular weekly meetings in a single
meeting room. War rooms provide dedicated physical spaces where
colocated teams can work together, collaborate spontaneously, and have
project information readily visible. Since colocation and meeting space
already exist, establishing a war room provides no additional benefit
and doesn\'t address the actual problems of interpersonal conflicts and
skill gaps. The real issues requiring attention are team member
conflicts and knowledge deficiencies, neither of which are solved by
providing a dedicated room when the team already has physical proximity
and meeting facilities.
erro_alternative2:
Training directly addresses the problem of three team members not
knowing how to complete their deliverables, building capabilities and
ensuring work quality through skill development and knowledge transfer.
erro_alternative3:
Reward and recognition can improve team morale and acknowledge positive
contributions, potentially reducing conflicts by creating a more
positive environment where team members feel valued and appreciated.
erro_alternative4:
Negotiation can help resolve conflicts between team members who are not
getting along by facilitating discussions where parties work toward
mutually acceptable solutions to their interpersonal issues.

statement:
A project manager is reviewing the stakeholder register to ensure it
contains all necessary information for effective stakeholder management.
The register currently includes various details about each identified
stakeholder. Which of the following would NOT be included in the stakeholder
register?
option1: Stakeholder influence level and interest in project outcomes
option2: Stakeholder requirements and expectations for project deliverables
option3: Specific communication methods and frequency for each stakeholder
option4: Stakeholder classification as internal or external to the organization
explanation:
Communication methods, frequency, and detailed approaches for each
stakeholder are documented in the communications management plan or
stakeholder engagement plan, not in the stakeholder register. The
register focuses on stakeholder identification, assessment, and
classification information that provides the foundation for developing
those plans. It captures who stakeholders are, their characteristics,
and their relationship to the project, but the detailed \"how\" of
engaging them is planned separately based on this foundational
information.
erro_alternative1:
Influence level and interest are core assessment information in the
stakeholder register, used to determine prioritization and engagement
strategies for different stakeholders.
erro_alternative2:
Requirements and expectations are essential stakeholder register
content, documenting what stakeholders need from the project and what
they anticipate receiving.
erro_alternative4:
Classification as internal or external is basic categorization
information captured in the stakeholder register along with other
classifications like supporter/neutral/resistor.

statement:
The trustworthiness of a message to its receivers may be prejudiced by
their level of trust in the communicator. This is an example of which of
the following?
option1: Filtering
option2: Encoding
option3: Noise
option4: Feedback
explanation:
Noise is anything that interferes with, distorts, or disrupts
communication between the sender and receiver, preventing the message
from being received or interpreted as intended. When receivers\' trust
levels in the communicator affect how they perceive or accept a message,
this represents psychological or perceptual noise in the communication
process. This type of noise creates a barrier between the sender and
receiver that has nothing to do with the actual content or accuracy of
the message but instead relates to the receiver\'s preconceptions,
biases, or past experiences with the sender. Noise can take many forms
including physical noise (environmental sounds), technical noise
(equipment problems), semantic noise (language or terminology barriers),
and psychological noise (emotions, attitudes, biases, trust issues). The
scenario describes psychological noise where lack of trust prejudices
the receivers against accepting the message regardless of its factual
accuracy, requiring the project manager to address the underlying trust
issues through relationship building, transparency, and consistent
reliable communication before message content will be received
objectively.
erro_alternative1:
Filtering occurs when the sender selectively communicates certain
information while withholding other information, or when intermediaries
modify messages as they pass through communication channels, but the
scenario describes receiver perception issues rather than sender
information selection or message modification.
erro_alternative2:
Encoding is the process by which the sender translates thoughts and
ideas into a message using words, symbols, or other communication
methods, but the scenario describes how receivers interpret the message
based on trust rather than how the sender formulates or encodes the
message.
erro_alternative4:
Feedback is the receiver\'s response to the sender\'s message,
completing the communication loop and allowing the sender to know
whether the message was received and understood correctly, but the
scenario describes factors affecting message reception rather than the
receiver\'s response mechanism.

statement:
When managing an agile project it is important to understand the needs
and views of the customers. The project team needs to ensure there is
clear agreement about when work items are truly complete and meet
customer expectations. What should the agile project team and project manager establish in
order to have a shared understanding between the team and the customers
about deliverable quality and completeness?
option1: Definition of done
option2: Definition of complete
option3: Definition of status
option4: Definition of finished product
explanation:
The definition of done is a formal description of the state of an
increment when it meets the quality measures required for the product,
creating shared understanding between the development team and customers
about what constitutes completed, acceptable work. This checklist of
criteria includes all conditions that must be satisfied for a product
increment to be considered complete, such as coding standards met, tests
passed, documentation updated, code reviewed, and acceptance criteria
satisfied. By establishing a clear definition of done, the agile team
and customers align their expectations about quality, completeness, and
readiness for delivery, preventing misunderstandings about when work is
truly finished versus merely functionally complete but lacking quality
attributes. The definition of done promotes transparency by making
completion criteria explicit and visible to all stakeholders, ensures
consistent quality across all increments by applying the same standards
throughout the project, and enables meaningful sprint reviews where
stakeholders can inspect potentially shippable increments that meet
agreed-upon quality standards rather than debating whether incomplete
work should be accepted.
erro_alternative2:
Definition of complete is not a standard agile term or artifact, and
while it sounds similar to definition of done, it lacks the formal
structure and shared agreement aspect that makes the definition of done
effective for creating alignment between teams and customers on quality
and completeness standards.
erro_alternative3:
Definition of status refers to reporting or tracking project progress
through status updates or status reports, but it does not establish the
shared understanding of completion criteria and quality standards that
align team and customer expectations about what constitutes acceptable
finished work.
erro_alternative4:
Definition of finished product might describe the final product
characteristics or requirements, but it does not provide the
increment-level completion criteria that guide teams and customers in
determining whether individual work items or sprint increments meet
quality standards and are ready for delivery or release.

statement:
You are overseeing an ERP implementation project that will cost the
company \$1 million and will take three years to complete. Six months
into the project, higher management decided to terminate your project as
they found an off-the-shelf solution that can be implemented in three
months at a fraction of the planned cost. The project is no longer
commercially viable for the company. What is the first thing you should do in this situation?
option1: Conduct scope verification.
option2: Update lessons learned documentation
