option3: Release project resources to other projects
option4: Archive project documents and records
explanation:
When a project is being terminated, even prematurely due to lack of
commercial viability, the first action should be to conduct scope
verification (also known as Validate Scope) to formally document and
obtain acceptance of the completed deliverables and work that was
performed up to the termination point. This process involves reviewing
work products with stakeholders, documenting what has been completed,
and obtaining formal acceptance of deliverables that meet acceptance
criteria, even if the overall project is not complete. Scope
verification during project closure ensures proper documentation of work
accomplished, provides formal recognition of value delivered before
termination, protects the project manager and team by documenting
completed work rather than allowing perception that nothing was
accomplished, and establishes the baseline for final project accounting
and resource release. Without scope verification, completed work may go
unrecognized, costs may not be properly attributed to delivered value,
and the organization may lose visibility into reusable components or
insights from the partial implementation.
erro_alternative2:
Updating lessons learned documentation is important during project
closure but should occur after scope verification confirms what work was
actually completed, as lessons learned should reflect the complete
picture of project performance including both completed deliverables and
reasons for termination.
erro_alternative3:
Releasing project resources to other projects should occur after scope
verification documents what work has been completed and ensures
resources are available to support final documentation and transition
activities, as premature resource release could leave incomplete
verification and handover processes.
erro_alternative4:
Archiving project documents and records is part of project closure but
should follow scope verification and other closure activities that
generate final documents needing preservation, as archiving incomplete
documentation could result in missing critical information about project
status at termination.

statement:
You are overseeing a software project to develop a new video game. Which
of the following statements is TRUE about your project?
option1:
All team members must be identified and committed during project
initiation
option2:
The project management plan cannot be updated once the executing phase
begins
option3:
Quality assurance activities only occur during the monitoring and
controlling phase
option4:
You will be acquiring several of your team members during the executing
phase.
explanation:
Acquire Resources occurs during the Executing Focus Area, where project
managers obtain team members, facilities, equipment, materials,
supplies, and other resources necessary to complete project work. While
some team members may be identified early during planning through
resource planning processes, the actual acquisition and assignment of
many team members typically occurs during execution as work packages are
ready to begin and resource availability aligns with project needs. This
is particularly true for software development projects where specialized
skills may be needed at different project phases, contractors or
consultants may join for specific development sprints or features, and
resource managers release staff to projects based on current workload
and priority. Acquiring resources during execution allows project
managers to secure the right skills at the right time based on actual
project progress rather than theoretical planning estimates, adjust
resource acquisition based on early project performance and lessons
learned, and respond to resource availability constraints that may not
have been visible during earlier planning activities.

statement:
While overseeing a new smartphone application development project, you
notice your team members are measuring the quality of an item on a
pass/fail basis. The team is checking whether each feature either meets
the acceptance criteria or does not meet the criteria, with no gradation
or measurement of degree. Which of the following methods are the team members using?
option1: Variable sampling
option2: Statistical process control
option3: Continuous measurement
option4: Attribute sampling
explanation:
Attribute sampling is a quality measurement method where items are
evaluated on a pass/fail, yes/no, or conforming/nonconforming basis
without measuring the degree of conformity or the magnitude of a
characteristic. When the team checks whether features meet or do not
meet acceptance criteria with no gradation or continuous measurement,
they are performing attribute sampling. This approach is appropriate
when quality characteristics can be clearly defined as meeting or not
meeting requirements, such as whether a button functions correctly,
whether data validates properly, or whether a feature performs as
specified. Attribute sampling is commonly used in software development
for binary checks like test case pass/fail results, defect
presence/absence, or requirement satisfaction verification. This method
simplifies quality measurement by eliminating the need to measure
degrees of conformance, provides clear binary decisions for acceptance
or rejection, and works well for characteristics where precise
measurement is unnecessary or where items either meet specifications or
require correction.
erro_alternative1:
Variable sampling measures characteristics on a continuous scale with
degrees of conformity rather than pass/fail decisions, such as measuring
response time in milliseconds, memory usage in megabytes, or performance
metrics with numeric values that can fall anywhere on a spectrum.
erro_alternative2:
Statistical process control is a quality management approach that uses
statistical methods and control charts to monitor process performance
over time and identify variations, but it does not specifically describe
the pass/fail binary measurement method the team is using.
erro_alternative3:
Continuous measurement evaluates characteristics on a continuous numeric
scale where values can fall anywhere within a range rather than using
binary pass/fail decisions, measuring attributes like load time, battery
consumption, or processing speed with specific numeric values.

statement:
A project manager is planning a research and development project where
the technical approach for later phases cannot be determined until
results from earlier experiments are known. The project sponsor asks why
the project manager is only creating detailed plans for the first three
months while leaving later work at a high level. Why would the project manager use rolling wave planning for this
project?
option1: To reduce the overall project planning effort and save time
option2: To defer detailed planning until more information becomes available
option3: To avoid commitment to specific deliverables or outcomes
option4: To allow team members flexibility in choosing their work
explanation:
Rolling wave planning is a progressive elaboration technique where
near-term work is planned in detail while future work remains at a
higher level until enough information is available to plan it
effectively. This approach is valuable when uncertainty exists about
future project phases, when requirements will emerge over time, or when
later work depends on outcomes of earlier work. As the project
progresses and uncertainty decreases, the project manager develops
detailed plans for upcoming work periods. This prevents wasted effort
planning details that will likely change based on new information.
erro_alternative1:
Rolling wave planning actually requires ongoing planning effort
throughout the project as each wave is detailed, rather than reducing
overall planning work or saving time.
erro_alternative3:
Rolling wave planning maintains commitment to project objectives and
deliverables but defers detailed planning of how those objectives will
be achieved until sufficient information exists.
erro_alternative4:
Rolling wave planning provides structured progressive planning rather
than giving team members unstructured flexibility to choose work,
maintaining project direction and coordination.

statement:
Which of the following is the characteristics of a high-performing agile
team?
option1: A team where each member specializes in only one technical area
option2: A team with members who are generalizing specialists
option3: A team that relies on detailed task assignments from project management
option4: A team that depends on external groups for most technical decisions
explanation:
Generalizing specialists, also known as T-shaped professionals, are team
members who have deep expertise in one or more specialties while also
possessing broad knowledge and skills across multiple disciplines. This
characteristic is fundamental to high-performing agile teams because it
enables team members to collaborate effectively across disciplines,
provides flexibility to work on different types of tasks as priorities
shift during iterations, and reduces dependencies and bottlenecks by
ensuring multiple team members can contribute to most work items. When
team members are generalizing specialists, the team can self-organize
more effectively because members understand enough about each other\'s
domains to coordinate work, provide meaningful input during planning and
reviews, and step in to help when bottlenecks occur. This breadth
combined with depth supports the agile principles of cross-functional
teams, sustainable pace, and continuous attention to technical
excellence while avoiding the silos and dependencies that slow down
specialized teams working in traditional structures.
erro_alternative1:
A team where each member specializes in only one technical area creates
silos and dependencies that slow agile teams, as work must wait for
specific specialists to be available rather than allowing team members
to collaborate and share work across disciplines to maintain flow and
meet iteration commitments.
erro_alternative3:
A team that relies on detailed task assignments from project management
contradicts the agile principle of self-organizing teams, as
high-performing agile teams determine among themselves how to accomplish
their work rather than waiting for external direction, which would slow
decision-making and reduce team empowerment.
erro_alternative4:
A team that depends on external groups for most technical decisions
lacks the autonomy and cross-functional capability needed for agile
effectiveness, as external dependencies create delays, reduce team
ownership of outcomes, and prevent the rapid feedback cycles that enable
agile teams to adapt quickly to changing requirements and technical
challenges.

statement:
You have been assigned as a project manager for an ongoing project and
asked to provide activity duration estimates as soon as possible. You
were surprised to find out that there was no detailed information
available on the project. You explored your organizational process
assets and identified a few similar projects that had been completed in
the past. Which one of the following will be the correct tool to utilize in this
kind of situation?
option1: Analogous Estimate
option2: Parametric Estimate
option3: Three-Point Estimate
option4: Bottom-Up Estimate
explanation:
Analogous estimating is a technique that uses the actual duration, cost,
or resource requirements of similar past projects as the basis for
estimating the current project, making it the most appropriate approach
when detailed project information is unavailable but historical data
from comparable projects exists in organizational process assets. This
top-down estimating approach is particularly valuable early in projects
or when time constraints require quick estimates before detailed
planning can occur, as it leverages organizational knowledge and lessons
learned from previous work to provide reasonable estimates with minimal
information requirements. Analogous estimating relies on expert judgment
to identify truly comparable projects and adjust estimates based on
known differences between past and current projects, such as team
experience, technology changes, or scope variations. While less accurate
than detailed bottom-up estimates, analogous estimating provides
sufficient accuracy for early planning decisions, budget allocation, and
schedule development when the alternative would be making commitments
with no estimation basis whatsoever, and estimates can be refined
through progressive elaboration as more detailed project information
becomes available.
erro_alternative2:
Parametric estimating requires a statistical relationship between
historical data and variables to calculate estimates using algorithms or
formulas, but this approach needs detailed quantitative data about
project parameters that is not available in this situation where
detailed information is lacking.
erro_alternative3:
Three-point estimating uses optimistic, pessimistic, and most likely
duration estimates to calculate expected duration and account for
uncertainty, but this technique requires sufficient project detail to
determine these three estimates, which is not available when detailed
project information does not exist.
erro_alternative4:
Bottom-up estimating builds detailed estimates by estimating individual
work packages or activities and aggregating them upward to create total
project estimates, but this approach requires the detailed work
breakdown and activity information that does not exist in the current
situation where detailed project information is unavailable.

statement:
A project manager is implementing statistical process control for a
manufacturing project to monitor production quality. The quality team is
setting up control charts to track process variations and determine when
corrective action is needed. The team needs to establish the upper and
lower control limits that will indicate whether the process is
performing within acceptable parameters or requires intervention.
option1:
The upper and lower control limits on a control chart are usually set
as:
option2: 1 Sigma, where 68.26 percent of occurrences will fall within the limits
option3: 2 Sigma, where 95.45 percent of occurrences will fall within the limits
option4: 3 Sigma, where 99.73 percent of occurrences will fall within the limits
option5:
6 Sigma, where 99.99966 percent of occurrences will fall within the
limits
explanation:
Control charts typically use 3 Sigma (three standard deviations from the
mean) as the standard for upper and lower control limits, meaning that
99.73 percent of process results should fall within these limits if the
process is in statistical control. This standard balances sensitivity to
process variations against the risk of false alarms, as setting limits
too narrow (1 or 2 Sigma) would trigger unnecessary investigations of
normal process variation, while setting limits too wide (beyond 3 Sigma)
might fail to detect actual process problems requiring correction. When
data points fall outside the 3 Sigma control limits, this signals that
special cause variation exists and the process is out of control,
requiring investigation and corrective action to identify and eliminate
the root cause. The 3 Sigma standard has become the convention in
quality management because it provides reasonable confidence that
variations within limits represent normal process behavior while
variations outside limits represent genuine problems, enabling project
managers to focus quality improvement efforts on actual process issues
rather than reacting to normal statistical variation.
erro_alternative1:
1 Sigma limits where 68.26 percent of occurrences fall within the limits
would be too narrow and would result in frequent false alarms where
normal process variation triggers unnecessary investigations, wasting
resources on perceived problems that represent expected statistical
variation rather than actual process issues.
erro_alternative2:
2 Sigma limits where 95.45 percent of occurrences fall within the limits
would still generate too many false positives for practical process
control, as approximately 4.5 percent of normal process results would
fall outside limits and trigger investigations of variation that does
not represent actual process problems.
erro_alternative3:
6 Sigma limits where 99.99966 percent of occurrences fall within the
limits would be too wide for standard control charts and would fail to
detect many process problems that should trigger corrective action,
though 6 Sigma is used as a process improvement methodology rather than
a control limit standard.

statement:
On an agile project, who should describe how the increments should be
tested?
option1: Product Owner
option2: Agile project team
option3: Scrum Master
option4: Quality Assurance Manager
explanation:
The agile project team, as a self-organizing and cross-functional unit,
has the responsibility and authority to determine how work will be
accomplished, including how increments will be tested to ensure they
meet quality standards and acceptance criteria. While the Product Owner
defines what needs to be built and the acceptance criteria that must be
met, the team determines the specific testing approaches, techniques,
tools, and strategies that will verify the increment satisfies those
criteria. This autonomy in determining how to accomplish work is
fundamental to agile principles, as team members possess the technical
expertise to make informed decisions about testing approaches
appropriate for the technology, risks, and quality requirements. The
team\'s responsibility for testing methods includes decisions about unit
testing, integration testing, automated test coverage, testing tools and
frameworks, and verification procedures that ensure increments are
potentially shippable and meet the definition of done established
collaboratively with stakeholders.
erro_alternative1:
Product Owner defines what needs to be built and the acceptance criteria
that must be met but does not determine how the team tests the
increments, as the Product Owner focuses on value delivery and
requirement prioritization rather than technical testing approaches that
require development and quality expertise.
erro_alternative3:
Scrum Master facilitates the team\'s work and helps remove impediments
but does not determine testing approaches, as the Scrum Master serves
the team by enabling their effectiveness rather than directing technical
decisions about how work should be accomplished, including testing
strategies.
erro_alternative4:
Quality Assurance Manager may exist in the broader organization but
should not dictate testing approaches to self-organizing agile teams, as
agile principles emphasize team autonomy in determining how to
accomplish work, though QA managers may provide guidance, tools, or
standards that teams can adapt to their specific context.

statement:
As the project team is collecting requirements for a software project,
the legal department of the organization informs them of a new recently
enacted law regarding data privacy that could affect the project. The
team determines that it is mandatory to implement compliance with this
new law in the project, as non-compliance would expose the organization
to legal liability and penalties. What is this?
option1: Constraint
option2: Assumption
option3: Risk
option4: Stakeholder requirement
explanation:
A constraint is a limiting factor that restricts or dictates the project
manager\'s options during planning and execution, and mandatory
compliance with laws and regulations represents a firm constraint that
cannot be negotiated or changed without organizational or legal
consequences. When a new law must be implemented in the project, it
establishes boundaries within which the project team must operate,
regardless of whether this constraint increases costs, extends schedule,
or reduces scope flexibility. Legal and regulatory compliance
constraints are particularly inflexible because they are imposed by
external authorities rather than internal stakeholders, making them
non-negotiable requirements that must be satisfied to avoid legal
liability, penalties, or operational shutdown. This constraint must be
documented in the project charter and incorporated into planning
processes across all performance domains, as it will influence scope
definition, technical approaches, resource requirements, budget
allocation, and risk management strategies throughout the project
lifecycle.
erro_alternative2:
Assumption is a factor considered true, real, or certain without proof
or demonstration for planning purposes, but mandatory compliance with
enacted laws is not an assumption requiring validation; it is a
confirmed constraint that definitely exists and must be satisfied
regardless of other project considerations.
erro_alternative3:
Risk is an uncertain event or condition that, if it occurs, has positive
or negative effects on project objectives, but mandatory legal
compliance is not uncertain; it is a definite constraint that must be
satisfied, though risks may arise from potential non-compliance or
difficulties achieving compliance within project constraints.
erro_alternative4:
Stakeholder requirement defines what the project must deliver or achieve
to satisfy stakeholder needs and expectations, but legal compliance is a
constraint that limits how the project can be executed rather than
defining what outcomes stakeholders desire, though compliance may
generate additional requirements for specific features or controls.

statement:
A project manager is leading an agile software development project for a
new mobile banking application. The team needs a way to better
understand the different types of users who will interact with the
application, their goals, motivations, and pain points. The Product
Owner wants to ensure the team develops features that create genuine
value for actual users rather than building functionality based on
assumptions about what users might want. What agile model can the customers and the project team use to help
describe the users of the software and how they would see value from the
software?
option1: User stories
option2: Product backlog
option3: Personas
option4: Use cases
explanation:
Personas are fictional but realistic representations of key user
segments that describe their goals, behaviors, motivations, pain points,
and contexts, helping the project team and customers develop shared
understanding of who will use the software and what value means from
different user perspectives. By creating personas that represent
different user types such as young tech-savvy professionals, elderly
customers unfamiliar with mobile banking, or small business owners
managing multiple accounts, the team can make design and prioritization
decisions based on how real users would perceive value rather than
abstract requirements disconnected from actual user needs. Personas
humanize users by giving them names, backgrounds, and realistic
characteristics that help team members empathize with user challenges
and opportunities, guide feature prioritization by focusing on
capabilities that deliver value to target personas, and improve
communication between technical team members and business stakeholders
by creating shared reference points for discussing user needs and value
delivery.
erro_alternative1:
User stories describe specific functionality or features from a user
perspective using the format \"As a \[user type\], I want \[goal\] so
that \[benefit\],\" but they describe what users want to accomplish
rather than describing the users themselves, their characteristics,
motivations, and how they perceive value across multiple interactions.
erro_alternative2:
Product backlog is a prioritized list of features, user stories,
enhancements, and fixes that need to be delivered, but it describes what
needs to be built rather than describing the users who will derive value
from those deliverables or how different user types perceive value
differently.
erro_alternative4:
Use cases describe interactions between users and systems to accomplish
specific goals through detailed step-by-step scenarios, but they focus
on functional requirements and system behavior rather than describing
user characteristics, motivations, contexts, and value perceptions that
inform design and prioritization decisions.

statement:
A project manager is analyzing the communication complexity of their
project. The project manager has calculated that there are 36 potential
communication channels among all team members, which helps determine the
effort required for stakeholder engagement and information distribution
throughout the project. How many members do you have in your team?
explanation:
The number of communication channels in a project is calculated using
the formula n(n-1)/2, where n represents the number of people who need
to communicate. When there are 36 communication channels, solving the
equation 36 = n(n-1)/2 yields n = 9 team members. This can be verified
by calculating 9(9-1)/2 = 9(8)/2 = 72/2 = 36 communication channels.
Understanding the relationship between team size and communication
channels is critical for project managers because communication
complexity grows exponentially rather than linearly as teams expand,
with each additional team member creating multiple new communication
paths that require management attention and coordination effort. This
mathematical relationship demonstrates why larger teams face
significantly greater communication challenges and why keeping teams
appropriately sized improves coordination effectiveness, reduces
miscommunication risks, and enhances team productivity by limiting
communication overhead that can consume substantial project time and
resources.
erro_alternative2:
8 team members would create only 28 communication channels using the
formula 8(8-1)/2 = 8(7)/2 = 56/2 = 28, which is fewer than the 36
channels identified in the project, indicating the team size must be
larger than 8 members.
erro_alternative3:
10 team members would create 45 communication channels using the formula
10(10-1)/2 = 10(9)/2 = 90/2 = 45, which exceeds the 36 channels
identified in the project, indicating the team size must be smaller than
10 members.
erro_alternative4:
12 team members would create 66 communication channels using the formula
12(12-1)/2 = 12(11)/2 = 132/2 = 66, which significantly exceeds the 36
channels identified in the project, indicating the team size must be
much smaller than 12 members.

statement:
The project manager decides to use an agile approach for a software
development project that was originally planned using a predictive
approach. The stakeholder engagement plan currently includes formal
monthly status reports submitted through department managers and
quarterly steering committee meetings for major decisions. The project
manager needs to revise the stakeholder engagement plan to align with
agile principles and practices. What should the project manager do to revise the stakeholder engagement
plan so that it follows agile principles?
option1:
Schedule stakeholder engagement activities only at the end of each
release cycle
option2:
Avoid delays by working directly with stakeholders rather than going
through management
option3:
Limit stakeholder involvement to designated Product Owner to reduce
interruptions
option4: Maintain formal documentation requirements but increase review frequency
explanation:
Agile principles emphasize direct collaboration between the project team
and stakeholders to enable rapid feedback, reduce decision delays, and
ensure the team builds what stakeholders actually need rather than what
intermediaries communicate stakeholders want. By working directly with
stakeholders rather than routing communication and decisions through
management layers, the project manager removes barriers that slow
feedback cycles, enables face-to-face conversations that improve
understanding and alignment, and supports the agile value of customer
collaboration over contract negotiation. Direct stakeholder engagement
allows the team to demonstrate working increments during sprint reviews,
gather immediate feedback on features and priorities, and adjust backlog
priorities based on stakeholder reactions to delivered functionality
rather than waiting for filtered feedback through management channels
that may introduce delays, misunderstandings, or distortions between
stakeholder needs and team understanding.
erro_alternative1:
Scheduling stakeholder engagement only at release cycle end contradicts
agile principles of frequent stakeholder collaboration and continuous
feedback, as agile approaches emphasize regular engagement through
sprint reviews, daily standups with key stakeholders when appropriate,
and ongoing communication rather than infrequent formal reviews.
erro_alternative3:
Limiting stakeholder involvement to designated Product Owner to reduce
interruptions creates a single point of failure and information
bottleneck that contradicts agile emphasis on broad stakeholder
collaboration, as effective agile projects engage multiple stakeholders
directly while the Product Owner synthesizes inputs and makes
prioritization decisions rather than serving as the sole stakeholder
interface.
erro_alternative4:
Maintaining formal documentation requirements but increasing review
frequency retains bureaucratic overhead that agile approaches minimize,
as agile principles favor working software and face-to-face conversation
over comprehensive documentation, though some documentation may still be
necessary based on organizational or regulatory requirements.

statement:
A project manager discovers that a newly assigned team member lacks the
technical skills required to perform their assigned tasks effectively.
The team member is willing to learn but cannot currently contribute at
the expected level, and this gap is affecting the project\'s progress. What should the project manager do first?
option1: Remove the team member from the project and request a replacement
option2: Assess the skill gap and develop a plan to address the deficiency
option3: Reassign the team member\'s work to other team members
option4: Escalate the issue to the functional manager immediately
explanation:
When discovering a skill gap, the project manager should first assess
the specific deficiencies and develop a plan to address them. This might
include training, mentoring, pairing with experienced team members, or
adjusting task assignments to match current capabilities while building
skills. Assessment determines the gap\'s severity, timeframe for
improvement, and appropriate interventions. This approach aligns with
the \"Build an Empowered Culture\" principle by investing in team member
development and supports the Lead the Team process by providing feedback
and managing team performance.
erro_alternative1:
Removing the team member immediately is premature without first
assessing whether the gap can be closed through training or support, and
may not be feasible depending on resource constraints.
erro_alternative3:
Reassigning work without assessment doesn\'t address the underlying
problem, overburdens other team members, and loses the opportunity to
develop the team member\'s capabilities.
erro_alternative4:
Escalating immediately bypasses the project manager\'s responsibility to
assess and attempt to resolve the issue, and may damage the team
member\'s confidence and relationship with management.

statement:
Key stakeholders submit a request to compress the project lifecycle from
16 months to 9 months due to competitive pressures. This represents a
significant schedule reduction that will impact multiple aspects of the
project. What should the project manager do first?
option1:
Analyze the impact on scope, cost, resources, quality, and risk before
responding
option2: Agree to the new timeline to maintain stakeholder satisfaction
option3: Reject the request because the original schedule was carefully planned
option4: Begin crashing activities on the critical path to meet the new deadline
explanation:
Before responding to any significant change request, the project manager
must conduct thorough impact analysis across all project constraints.
Compressing a schedule by 44% (from 16 to 9 months) will have
substantial implications for scope achievability, budget requirements
(crashing or fast tracking costs), resource needs, quality standards,
and project risks. This analysis provides stakeholders with the
information needed to make informed decisions about whether the
compression is feasible, what trade-offs are acceptable, and what
additional resources or scope reductions might be necessary. The
analysis should go through the Assess and Implement Changes process.
erro_alternative2:
Agreeing to the timeline without analysis commits to a deadline that may
be impossible to meet and sets the project up for failure without
understanding feasibility or required trade-offs.
erro_alternative3:
Rejecting the request outright dismisses potentially valid business
needs without exploring whether compression is achievable with
appropriate adjustments to other constraints.
erro_alternative4:
Beginning to crash activities assumes compression is both feasible and
approved, bypassing required impact analysis and stakeholder
decision-making about trade-offs and additional costs.

statement:
During a sprint, an Agile team is falling significantly behind on a
critical task that is essential for meeting the sprint goal. The team
realizes they will not be able to complete this work by the end of the
sprint at their current pace. What should the project manager do?
option1:
Extend the sprint duration to allow the team to complete the critical
task
option2:
Help the team identify and remove impediments blocking progress on the
task
option3: Assign additional team members from other projects to complete the task
option4:
Move the critical task to the next sprint and continue with remaining
work
explanation:
When an Agile team is falling behind, the project manager\'s (or Scrum
Master\'s) primary role is to identify and remove impediments blocking
the team\'s progress. Impediments might include external dependencies,
resource constraints, technical blockers, unclear requirements, or
organizational obstacles. By facilitating impediment removal, the
project manager enables the team to focus on delivering value. This
supports the \"Build an Empowered Culture\" principle by removing
barriers rather than dictating solutions. The team should also
collaborate to determine if work can be adjusted or if they need to
communicate with the product owner about scope adjustments for the
current sprint.
erro_alternative1:
Extending the sprint duration violates the timeboxed nature of sprints,
which are fixed-length iterations that create delivery rhythm and enable
predictable planning.
erro_alternative3:
Assigning additional team members disrupts team dynamics, requires
onboarding time that may not help in the short term, and doesn\'t
address the root cause of why the team is behind.
erro_alternative4:
Moving the critical task to the next sprint abandons the sprint goal and
doesn\'t address why the team fell behind, potentially repeating the
same problems in future sprints.

statement:
A project manager is conducting an iteration review for a software
development project using an agile approach. During the review, several
stakeholders raise various complaints about the delivered increment,
including concerns about functionality, usability, and how well the
features align with their expectations. The project manager needs to
determine the appropriate first response to these stakeholder concerns. What should the project manager do first in order to address this issue?
option1: Schedule individual meetings with each complaining stakeholder
option2: Evaluate the complaints before addressing them
option3: Update the product backlog to reflect stakeholder feedback
option4: Communicate the complaints to the development team immediately
explanation:
Before taking any action to address stakeholder complaints, the project
manager must first evaluate the complaints to understand their validity,
root causes, priority, and potential impact on project objectives and
value delivery. This evaluation should determine whether complaints
reflect genuine deficiencies in delivered functionality, misalignment
between stakeholder expectations and acceptance criteria,
miscommunication about what would be delivered in the iteration, or
stakeholder misunderstanding of features that actually work as intended.
By evaluating complaints systematically, the project manager can
categorize issues by severity and type, identify patterns that suggest
systemic problems versus isolated concerns, distinguish between critical
defects requiring immediate correction and enhancement requests that
should be prioritized in the backlog, and develop appropriate response
strategies tailored to each complaint category. This analytical approach
prevents reactive responses that may waste effort addressing
low-priority concerns while missing critical issues, and demonstrates
the principle of adopting a holistic view by considering complaints in
the broader context of project objectives, stakeholder needs, and value
delivery before committing to specific corrective actions.
erro_alternative1:
Scheduling individual meetings with each complaining stakeholder may be
appropriate after evaluation reveals that deeper understanding of
specific concerns is needed, but conducting these meetings before
evaluating complaints risks consuming significant time on issues that
may not warrant individual attention or may be resolved through simpler
approaches once properly understood and categorized.
erro_alternative3:
Updating the product backlog to reflect stakeholder feedback should
occur after evaluation determines which complaints represent legitimate
defects requiring immediate correction, which represent new requirements
or enhancements for future iterations, and which represent
misunderstandings that need clarification rather than backlog changes,
as premature backlog updates without evaluation may introduce
unnecessary work items.
erro_alternative4:
Communicating complaints to the development team immediately without
first evaluating them risks demoralizing the team with unfiltered
criticism that may include invalid complaints or misunderstandings, and
prevents the project manager from providing the team with analyzed,
categorized feedback that helps them understand priority and root causes
rather than reacting defensively to raw stakeholder complaints.

statement:
One of your project team members expresses concerns about the project
management plan. The project manager is surprised because the team is
currently working on an important aspect of the project and this issue
has not come up before. The project manager needs to determine the
appropriate response to this unexpected concern. What steps should the project manager take?
option1:
Continue with current work since the project is progressing well and
address the concern later
option2: Immediately convene a team meeting to discuss and resolve the concern
option3:
Revise the project management plan to address the team member\'s
feedback
option4:
Immediately acknowledge the team member\'s concern but don\'t make any
decisions immediately. First meet with the team member one-on-one
explanation:
The project manager should immediately acknowledge the concern to
demonstrate respect for the team member and encourage open
communication, then meet one-on-one to fully understand the issue before
making decisions. This approach allows the project manager to gather
complete information about the concern\'s nature, scope, and validity
without premature reactions that could disrupt team dynamics or create
unnecessary alarm. A private conversation enables candid discussion,
helps determine whether the concern represents a genuine project risk
requiring action or a misunderstanding requiring clarification, and
prevents potential embarrassment or defensiveness that might occur in
group settings. Only after understanding the issue can the project
manager determine appropriate next steps such as plan revisions, team
discussions, or stakeholder communications.
erro_alternative1:
Continuing with current work while deferring the concern dismisses the
team member\'s input and may allow a legitimate issue to escalate,
potentially damaging team trust and missing opportunities to address
problems before they impact project outcomes.
erro_alternative2:
Immediately convening a team meeting risks wasting team time if the
concern is minor or based on misunderstanding, may create unnecessary
alarm about project management plan adequacy, and prevents the project
manager from understanding the issue privately before deciding whether
broader team involvement is warranted.
erro_alternative3:
Revising the project management plan immediately assumes the concern is
valid and requires plan changes before the project manager understands
the issue fully, potentially creating unnecessary rework or disruption
if the concern stems from miscommunication rather than actual plan
deficiencies.

statement:
During a meeting, an agile project manager is informed that the project
must be delivered much sooner than planned. The project manager is also
informed that there are no additional technical resources available for
the accelerated timeline. However, the project manager knows the
technical resource manager fairly well and has a good working
relationship with them. What is the first thing the project manager should do?
option1: Negotiate with stakeholders to reduce scope to meet the new deadline
option2: Re-prioritize the product backlog to focus on highest-value features
option3: Ask the technical resource manager what resources are available
option4: Fast track the project schedule to compress the timeline
explanation:
The project manager should verify resource availability directly with
the technical resource manager rather than accepting secondhand
information about resource constraints. This direct communication
leverages the existing relationship to gather accurate, current
information about actual resource availability, constraints, and
potential options that may not have been communicated through
intermediaries. The person who informed the project manager about
resource unavailability may not have complete information, may have
outdated information, or may not have explored all possibilities with
the resource manager. By going directly to the source, the project
manager can understand the true resource situation, discuss creative
solutions like temporary reassignments or part-time allocations, and
make informed decisions based on facts rather than assumptions.
erro_alternative1:
Negotiating scope reduction assumes resource constraints are absolute
before verifying actual resource availability, potentially sacrificing
project value unnecessarily if resources could actually be obtained
through direct discussion with the resource manager.
erro_alternative2:
Re-prioritizing the product backlog may be necessary eventually but
should follow resource verification, as backlog priorities should be
based on actual constraints rather than assumed limitations that may not
reflect reality.
erro_alternative4:
Fast tracking the schedule is a schedule compression technique that may
be appropriate but should only be considered after confirming resource
constraints are genuine and exploring whether additional resources could
eliminate the need for schedule compression that introduces risk.

statement:
The project manager decides that a project will be best served by an
agile approach. Unfortunately, some of the team members do not have
experience with agile methodologies and are unfamiliar with agile
practices, ceremonies, and frameworks. The project manager needs to
ensure the team can effectively execute using the chosen approach. How should the project manager respond?
option1:
Request experienced agile team members from other projects to replace
inexperienced members
option2: Hire external agile consultants to guide the team through the project
option3:
Proceed with the project and have team members learn agile practices on
the job
option4: Organize cost-efficient agile training for team members
explanation:
The project manager should invest in training existing team members to
build organizational capability and ensure the team can execute the
agile approach effectively. Cost-efficient training options include
internal knowledge sharing from experienced practitioners, online
courses, workshops, or brief external training sessions that provide
foundational knowledge. Training the existing team builds long-term
organizational capability, demonstrates commitment to team development,
and ensures all members understand agile principles and practices needed
for effective collaboration. This approach aligns with the principle of
building an empowered culture by investing in team member growth and
capability development.
erro_alternative1:
Requesting experienced agile team members to replace inexperienced
members wastes the opportunity to develop organizational capability, may
not be feasible due to resource constraints on other projects, and
damages team morale by suggesting the organization abandons team members
who lack specific skills rather than investing in their development.
erro_alternative2:
Hiring external agile consultants creates dependency on external
resources, incurs ongoing costs throughout the project, and fails to
build internal capability that would benefit future projects, though
consultants may be valuable supplements to training rather than
replacements for team development.
erro_alternative3:
Proceeding without training and expecting team members to learn on the
job risks project failure due to lack of foundational knowledge, creates
inefficiency through trial-and-error learning, and may result in poor
agile implementation that undermines stakeholder confidence in the
approach.

statement:
A project manager feels that a delay in creating a prototype was caused
by missing skills on the team. The team has diverse technical
backgrounds, but the project manager did not thoroughly assess
individual capabilities before assigning work. The delay has impacted
the project schedule and the project manager wants to prevent similar
situations in future iterations. How could the project manager avoid this situation in the future?
option1:
Provide comprehensive training to all team members on prototype
development
option2:
Identify team member strengths, and organize the team to take advantage
of those strengths
option3:
Request additional skilled resources from functional managers for future
prototyping work
option4:
Extend task durations in the schedule to account for potential skill
gaps
explanation:
The project manager should assess each team member\'s technical skills,
experience, and strengths, then organize work assignments to leverage
those capabilities effectively. This approach maximizes team
productivity by matching tasks to people with appropriate skills rather
than forcing team members to work outside their competencies. By
understanding team strengths upfront during resource planning, the
project manager can assign work strategically, identify actual skill
gaps that require training or external resources, and create
opportunities for knowledge sharing where experienced members mentor
others. This proactive resource management prevents delays by ensuring
the right people work on tasks matching their capabilities.
erro_alternative1:
Providing comprehensive training to all team members is expensive,
time-consuming, and unnecessary if existing team members already possess
needed skills that simply weren\'t identified or utilized effectively
through appropriate work assignments.
erro_alternative3:
Requesting additional skilled resources assumes the team lacks necessary
capabilities without first assessing whether existing team members
possess the needed skills but weren\'t assigned appropriately based on
their strengths and experience.
erro_alternative4:
Extending task durations accepts inefficiency rather than addressing the
root cause, adds unnecessary schedule padding that increases project
costs, and fails to leverage team capabilities that could complete work
efficiently if properly assigned.

statement:
A project manager discovers that the team\'s daily standup meetings
consistently run over the 15-minute timebox because team members spend
excessive time discussing problems and obstructions in detail. Instead
of brief status updates and impediment identification, the team engages
in lengthy problem-solving discussions that involve only a few members
while others wait. How should the project manager handle the situation?
option1:
Eliminate standup meetings and rely on asynchronous status updates
instead
option2:
Extend the standup timebox to 30 minutes to accommodate problem
discussions
option3: Remove impediment discussions from standups and address them separately
option4: Ask the team members to be more efficient with their time in meetings
explanation:
The project manager should coach team members to keep standup meetings
focused and efficient by identifying problems without solving them
during the standup. The purpose of the daily standup is to synchronize
work, surface impediments, and identify who needs to collaborate, not to
solve problems with the entire team present. Team members should note
impediments during the standup then address them immediately afterward
in smaller groups involving only relevant people. By reminding the team
to be more efficient, the project manager reinforces the standup\'s
intended purpose while preserving its value for coordination without
eliminating necessary problem-solving that should occur outside the
timebox.
erro_alternative1:
Eliminating standup meetings removes a valuable synchronization
mechanism and throws out the solution rather than addressing the misuse
of the meeting format, losing the coordination benefits standups provide
when conducted properly.
erro_alternative2:
Extending the standup timebox to accommodate problem discussions
legitimizes inefficient use of team time by having everyone attend
detailed discussions that only involve a few members, violating the
principle that standups should be brief coordination meetings.
erro_alternative3:
Removing impediment discussions from standups eliminates a core purpose
of the meeting, as identifying impediments is essential to the
standup\'s value, though solving those impediments should occur
afterward with relevant people rather than during the standup itself.

statement:
A customer complains that a deliverable does not meet their expectations
several iterations into a software development project. The project
manager is surprised since the team has been delivering increments
regularly. Upon investigation, the project manager discovers the
customer attended few iteration reviews and did not provide feedback
during those sessions. What is one way that the project manager could have avoided this result?
option1: Create more detailed requirements documentation at project initiation
option2: Increase the frequency of formal status reports to the customer
option3: Double-check that the customer understands the value of iterative review
option4: Extend iteration durations to allow more complete deliverables
explanation:
The project manager should have ensured the customer understood the
importance of participating in iteration reviews and providing feedback
on increments. Many customers unfamiliar with iterative approaches may
not realize that their active participation and feedback during reviews
is essential to ensuring the final deliverable meets expectations. By
educating the customer about the value of iterative review, the project
manager creates shared understanding that increments are opportunities
to inspect progress, validate direction, and adjust requirements before
significant work is completed. This prevents the common problem where
customers treat iteration reviews as passive information sessions rather
than active collaboration opportunities to shape the evolving product.
erro_alternative1:
Creating more detailed requirements documentation assumes requirements
can be fully known upfront, which contradicts iterative approaches where
requirements emerge and evolve through feedback on working increments,
and detailed documentation doesn\'t replace active customer engagement
in reviews.
erro_alternative2:
Increasing formal status report frequency provides information about
progress but doesn\'t enable the interactive feedback and course
correction that iteration reviews provide through demonstrating working
functionality and gathering customer reactions.
erro_alternative4:
Extending iteration durations produces larger increments less
frequently, which actually reduces feedback opportunities and increases
the risk that work proceeds in wrong directions for longer periods
before customer feedback reveals misalignment.

statement:
A project manager\'s team has been efficiently managing budget and scope
to accomplish expected results. However, market changes are increasing
the project's need for new skills. What can the project manager do to meet this new demand?
option1:
Continue with the current team and hope they can learn the skills during
execution
option2:
Delay the project until team members complete extensive training
programs
option3: Reduce project scope to eliminate work requiring the new skills
option4: Acquire resources with the needed skills to supplement the existing team
explanation:
When new skills become essential due to changing requirements, the
project manager should acquire resources who possess those capabilities
through the Acquire Resources process. This might involve hiring new
team members, contracting specialists, or obtaining resources from other
parts of the organization. Bringing in skilled resources enables the
project to address new requirements effectively while existing team
members can learn from the new experts. This approach balances immediate
project needs with long-term team development and aligns with adapting
to market changes rather than ignoring them.
erro_alternative1:
Hoping team members can learn complex new skills while simultaneously
executing project work is unrealistic and puts the project at
significant risk of failure or quality issues.
erro_alternative2:
Delaying the project for extensive training ignores the market pressures
that created the urgency for these new capabilities and may cause the
project to miss critical business opportunities.
erro_alternative3:
Reducing scope to eliminate new skill requirements means the project
won\'t address the market changes that made these capabilities
necessary, potentially delivering a product that doesn\'t meet current
market needs.

statement:
A new project manager has been assigned to take over a project that is
currently in the execution phase. The team members know each other well
and have been working together for some time. Recently, there have been
complaints from a team member about how the project is being managed
under the new project manager. The complaints suggest friction between
the team\'s established working style and the new manager\'s approach. How should this new project manager respond?
option1:
Meet one-on-one with the complaining team member to understand their
specific concerns
option2:
Require team members to attend a meeting with the goal of creating a new
social contract
option3:
Document the complaints and monitor team performance for additional
issues
option4: Continue with current management approach since the team is experienced
explanation:
A social contract (also called team charter or working agreement)
establishes how the team will work together, make decisions, resolve
conflicts, and interact with leadership. When a new project manager
joins an established team, creating or revisiting the social contract
allows the team and new manager to align on expectations, working norms,
and collaboration approaches. This collaborative process addresses
underlying issues about management style through shared agreement rather
than top-down imposition, creates psychological safety for team members
to voice concerns constructively, and establishes clear guidelines that
prevent future friction. The social contract development process itself
demonstrates the principle of building an empowered culture by involving
the team in defining how they will be managed.
erro_alternative1:
Meeting one-on-one addresses individual concerns but doesn\'t resolve
the broader team alignment issue that likely affects multiple members,
and private conversations may not surface systemic problems between the
new manager\'s approach and team expectations.
erro_alternative3:
Documenting complaints and monitoring performance is passive and allows
issues to persist rather than proactively addressing the disconnect
between the new manager\'s approach and team working preferences through
collaborative norm-setting.
erro_alternative4:
Continuing with current management approach ignores legitimate team
concerns and assumes the new manager\'s approach is correct without
considering whether adaptation to the team\'s established dynamics would
improve effectiveness and team satisfaction.

statement:
During a heavily-regulated pharmaceutical project, the project sponsor
and a high-level organizational decision-maker have requested changes to
the clinical trial protocol that appear to bypass regulatory compliance
requirements. The changes would accelerate the timeline significantly,
but the project manager suspects they may violate FDA regulations
governing patient safety and data integrity. What should be the first step in the project manager\'s response?
option1: Implement the requested changes since they come from senior leadership
option2: Refuse to implement the changes and document the decision
option3: Seek in-house legal advice
option4: Report the request to the regulatory agency immediately
explanation:
The project manager should immediately consult the organization\'s legal
counsel to verify whether the requested changes actually violate
regulations before taking further action. What appears to be regulatory
non-compliance to the project manager may have legal nuances or
interpretations that counsel can clarify. Legal advice provides the
project manager with authoritative guidance on regulatory obligations,
potential consequences of non-compliance, and appropriate responses to
the request. This protects both the organization and the project manager
by ensuring decisions are based on legal expertise rather than
assumptions about regulatory requirements.
erro_alternative1:
Implementing changes from senior leadership without verifying regulatory
compliance exposes the organization to legal liability, regulatory
penalties, and potential harm to trial participants, regardless of who
requested the changes.
erro_alternative2:
Refusing to implement changes without first confirming they violate
regulations may be premature if legal counsel determines the changes are
actually compliant, and outright refusal without legal backing may
damage relationships unnecessarily.
erro_alternative4:
Reporting the request to the regulatory agency immediately is premature
before confirming a violation exists and bypasses internal resolution
processes that should occur first, potentially creating regulatory
scrutiny that may be unwarranted if the changes are actually compliant.

statement:
A project manager is working on several projects simultaneously. For one
project, the project manager is just starting with a new team that has
not worked together before. Unfortunately, the project manager\'s other
critical duties will keep them from focusing full attention on the new
project for a few weeks during the initiation phase. How should the project manager handle the situation?
option1: Foster team cohesion by facilitating the creation of a social contract
option2:
Delay project initiation until the project manager can dedicate full
attention
option3: Assign an experienced team member to act as interim project manager
option4: Request that the sponsor postpone the project start date
explanation:
Creating a social contract (team charter or working agreement) helps the
new team establish working norms, decision-making processes,
communication methods, and conflict resolution approaches that enable
them to function effectively even when the project manager has limited
availability. The social contract development process builds team
cohesion by having members collaboratively define how they will work
together, creates shared ownership of team dynamics and performance, and
establishes guidelines that reduce dependency on constant project
manager presence. By facilitating this upfront, the project manager
enables team self-organization and empowers members to collaborate
productively during periods of reduced project manager availability.
erro_alternative2:
Delaying project initiation creates schedule impacts and may not be
acceptable to stakeholders who expect the project to start as planned,
and the delay doesn\'t address the underlying issue of limited project
manager availability.
erro_alternative3:
Assigning an interim project manager creates confusion about authority
and accountability, may not be feasible if no experienced team member is
available, and introduces leadership instability during the critical
team formation phase.
erro_alternative4:
Requesting postponement from the sponsor acknowledges the constraint but
doesn\'t solve it, as the project manager\'s competing duties may
continue, and sponsors generally expect project managers to handle
multiple projects simultaneously through effective delegation and team
empowerment.

statement:
A project manager is leading a product development project when
stakeholders express concern that their urgent business needs may not be
addressed in time due to the sequential nature of the project plan. The
stakeholders need certain high-priority features delivered quickly to
respond to competitive pressures, but the current plan shows these
features scheduled for later phases. What should the project manager do?
option1:
Reassure stakeholders that all features will be delivered according to
the original plan
option2:
Work with stakeholders to prioritize features and deliver high-value
items early
option3: Compress the schedule by crashing critical path activities
option4: Request additional budget to accelerate all project work
explanation:
When stakeholders have urgent business needs, the project manager should
collaborate with them to prioritize deliverables based on business value
and deliver the most critical features first. This might involve
adopting a more iterative or incremental approach, restructuring the
project plan to front-load high-priority items, or implementing phased
delivery. This approach aligns with the \"Focus on Value\" principle by
ensuring the most important capabilities are delivered when they\'re
needed most, even if other features come later. It also maintains
stakeholder engagement by demonstrating responsiveness to their business
concerns.
erro_alternative1:
Reassuring stakeholders without addressing their concerns ignores
legitimate business needs and damages trust by appearing inflexible to
changing priorities or market conditions.
erro_alternative3:
Compressing the entire schedule through crashing increases costs
significantly while still delivering everything at the end rather than
providing early access to urgent high-priority features.
erro_alternative4:
Requesting additional budget to accelerate all work is expensive and
unnecessary when stakeholders only need specific high-priority features
quickly, not the entire project accelerated.

statement:
A project manager is reviewing the resource management plan and realizes
that the resources currently allocated to the project may not be
sufficient to complete all planned work within the approved schedule and
budget constraints. The project is about to move from planning into
execution. What should the project manager do?
option1: Begin execution and request additional resources when shortages occur
option2: Reduce the project team size to work within current allocations
option3:
Conduct resource analysis and present findings to stakeholders for
decision-making
option4: Extend the project schedule to match available resource capacity
explanation:
When resource adequacy is uncertain, the project manager should conduct
thorough resource analysis to quantify the gap between what\'s needed
and what\'s available, assess the impact on schedule and scope, and
evaluate options for addressing the shortfall. This analysis should be
presented to stakeholders who can make informed decisions about whether
to secure additional resources, adjust scope, extend the schedule, or
accept increased risk. The analysis enables data-driven decision-making
rather than assumptions and ensures stakeholders understand trade-offs
before committing to execution.
erro_alternative1:
Beginning execution without addressing known resource concerns sets the
project up for failure and creates crisis situations when shortages
materialize, making solutions more expensive and disruptive.
erro_alternative2:
Reducing team size when resources are already potentially insufficient
worsens the problem rather than solving it and makes project success
even less likely.
erro_alternative4:
Extending the schedule unilaterally assumes this is the acceptable
solution without consulting stakeholders who may prefer different
trade-offs like reduced scope or additional budget for resources.

statement:
A project manager notices one team member is not making progress
according to schedule, and this lack of progress is now at risk of
delaying the critical path. During a conversation, the team member
expresses that they feel they are not the best resource to fill their
currently assigned role and lack confidence in their ability to complete
the work successfully. How should the project manager handle the situation?
option1: Request a replacement resource from the functional manager immediately
option2:
After reassessing the strengths and weaknesses of the team member,
discuss the best areas for them to contribute
option3:
Provide intensive training to bring the team member up to required skill
level
option4: Keep the team member in the current role but assign a mentor for support
explanation:
The project manager should reassess the team member\'s actual
capabilities, strengths, and weaknesses, then have a collaborative
discussion about where they can contribute most effectively on the
project. The team member\'s self-assessment that they\'re not the best
fit suggests a potential mismatch between skills and assignment rather
than lack of capability. By reassessing strengths, the project manager
may identify other critical project needs that better align with the
team member\'s abilities, enabling them to contribute successfully in a
different role while addressing the critical path delay through better
resource alignment. This approach respects the team member\'s
self-awareness, leverages their actual strengths rather than forcing
them into ill-fitting roles, and may reveal capabilities that weren\'t
initially recognized.
erro_alternative1:
Requesting immediate replacement wastes the opportunity to leverage the
team member\'s actual strengths in a different capacity, may not be
feasible due to resource availability constraints, and damages morale by
giving up on the team member without exploring reassignment options.
erro_alternative3:
Providing intensive training addresses skill gaps but may not resolve
the issue if the fundamental mismatch is between the team member\'s
natural strengths and the role requirements, and training takes time
that may not be available given the critical path risk.
erro_alternative4:
Keeping the team member in the current role with mentoring support
doesn\'t address the stated concern that they\'re not the best fit for
the role, and adding mentoring overhead may further delay progress if
the underlying issue is skill mismatch rather than lack of guidance.

statement:
A product owner is complaining that the project does not meet
agreed-upon parameters regarding functionality and quality. The team has
completed three iterations, but the product owner expresses
dissatisfaction with how the increments align with their expectations
and the acceptance criteria that were established at the project start. How should the project manager handle the situation?
option1:
Review the project charter with the product owner to confirm original
requirements
option2: Update the project management plan to reflect new parameters
option3: Escalate the issue to the project sponsor for resolution
option4:
Schedule time with the product owner to attend a retrospective session
and discuss all corrective actions
explanation:
The retrospective provides a structured forum for the team and product
owner to openly discuss what\'s working, what\'s not working, and what
corrective actions are needed to improve alignment with agreed-upon
parameters. This collaborative approach involves the product owner
directly in problem-solving, surfaces root causes of the disconnect
between expectations and deliverables, and generates actionable
improvements through team consensus. The retrospective format encourages
honest dialogue in a psychologically safe environment where participants
focus on process improvement rather than blame, enabling the team and
product owner to identify specific corrective actions that address the
underlying issues causing misalignment.
erro_alternative1:
Reviewing the project charter confirms original requirements but
doesn\'t address the current execution problems or create a path forward
for improving alignment, and focuses on documentation rather than
collaborative problem-solving.
erro_alternative2:
Updating the project management plan may be necessary eventually but
should follow the retrospective that identifies what changes are needed,
as updating the plan without understanding root causes risks
implementing ineffective solutions.
erro_alternative3:
Escalating to the sponsor bypasses the project manager\'s responsibility
to resolve team-level issues collaboratively and signals that the
project manager cannot manage stakeholder concerns without executive
intervention.

statement:
A project manager is leading a software development project during the
execution phase when a key stakeholder submits a request to add a new
feature that was not included in the original scope. The stakeholder
believes this feature would significantly enhance the product\'s market
competitiveness. What should the project manager do?
option1: Add the feature immediately since it comes from a key stakeholder
option2:
Evaluate the impact and submit the request through the change control
process
option3: Reject the request to protect the approved project scope
option4: Ask the development team if they can accommodate the additional work
explanation:
All scope changes during execution must go through the established
change control process, regardless of the requester\'s position or the
perceived value of the change. The project manager should evaluate how
the new feature would impact scope, schedule, cost, resources, quality,
and risk, then submit this analysis through the Assess and Implement
Changes process for proper review and approval. This ensures
stakeholders make informed decisions about whether the feature\'s value
justifies its impacts, maintains project governance, and prevents
uncontrolled scope expansion even when changes seem beneficial.
erro_alternative1:
Adding features immediately because of stakeholder seniority bypasses
required governance and creates uncontrolled scope creep that can
jeopardize project objectives and baselines.
erro_alternative3:
Rejecting the request without evaluation dismisses potentially valuable
improvements and damages stakeholder relationships when the change might
be justified after proper impact analysis.
erro_alternative4:
Asking the team about accommodation focuses only on feasibility without
conducting proper impact analysis across all constraints or obtaining
required approvals from appropriate authority levels.

statement:
A project manager is leading a product development project when a key
customer expresses serious concerns about the technical approach the
team plans to use. The customer believes a different approach would
better meet their long-term needs and is worried the current direction
will create problems. What should the project manager do?
option1:
Continue with the planned approach since technical decisions are the
team\'s responsibility
option2:
Meet with the customer to understand their concerns and evaluate the
technical options
option3: Escalate the issue to the project sponsor for a final decision
option4: Conduct a risk assessment of the current technical approach
explanation:
When a customer raises concerns about technical approach, the project
manager should engage directly to understand the underlying issues,
business drivers, and specific concerns. This conversation may reveal
important requirements or constraints that weren\'t fully understood,
legitimate technical risks the team overlooked, or misunderstandings
that can be clarified. After understanding the concerns, the project
manager can work with the technical team to evaluate options and
determine whether the current approach is sound or if adjustments are
warranted. This aligns with customer collaboration and stakeholder
engagement principles.
erro_alternative1:
Dismissing customer concerns because technical decisions belong to the
team ignores valuable stakeholder input and risks building a product
that doesn\'t meet customer needs or expectations.
erro_alternative3:
Escalating immediately bypasses the project manager\'s responsibility
for stakeholder management and may unnecessarily elevate what could be
resolved through direct engagement and technical discussion.
erro_alternative4:
Conducting risk assessment addresses only part of the issue without
first understanding the customer\'s specific concerns, which may reveal
requirements gaps or misunderstandings rather than just risks.

statement:
A project manager discovers that there is an issue with some of the
project documents on a hybrid project, such as outdated information in
the project management plan or incorrect stakeholder contact details in
the communications plan. The team is currently in the third iteration
and focused on delivering sprint commitments. How should the project manager handle the situation?
option1: Schedule a team meeting to discuss the document issues
option2: Wait until the retrospective to address the documentation issues
option3: Immediately fix the issue independently of the team
option4: Escalate the documentation issue to the sponsor
explanation:
The project manager should immediately fix documentation issues
independently without involving the team, as these are typically project
management responsibilities that don\'t require team input or
collaboration. Issues with project documents like the project management
plan, stakeholder registers, or communications plans are the project
manager\'s domain and shouldn\'t distract the team from value delivery
work. By handling documentation corrections independently, the project
manager demonstrates accountable leadership by taking ownership of their
responsibilities, protects the team\'s focus on iteration work, and
resolves the issue efficiently without unnecessary meetings or delays.
erro_alternative1:
Scheduling a team meeting wastes team time on administrative
documentation issues that don\'t require their participation,
distracting them from delivering iteration commitments and creating
unnecessary overhead for problems the project manager can resolve
independently.
erro_alternative2:
Waiting until the retrospective delays fixing a documentation issue
unnecessarily, as retrospectives focus on process improvement and team
collaboration issues rather than administrative document corrections
that the project manager should handle immediately.
erro_alternative4:
Escalating documentation issues to the sponsor is inappropriate for
problems within the project manager\'s authority and responsibility to
resolve, as sponsors expect project managers to handle routine project
management tasks independently without escalation.

statement:
A project manager is nearing the end of an iteration when a key customer
threatens to reject the deliverables, stating they don\'t meet
expectations. The team has been working according to the defined
requirements and believes the deliverables are complete. What should the project manager do first?
option1: Complete the iteration and address concerns in the next iteration
option2:
Meet with the customer immediately to understand their specific concerns
and expectations
option3:
Review the requirements documentation to confirm the team built what was
specified
option4: Ask the team to make quick modifications to satisfy the customer
explanation:
When a customer threatens to reject deliverables, immediate engagement
is critical to understand the disconnect between what was delivered and
what they expected. This meeting should clarify specific concerns,
identify which aspects don\'t meet expectations, and determine whether
the issue stems from misunderstood requirements, evolving needs, quality
problems, or miscommunication. Understanding the root cause enables
appropriate corrective action. This demonstrates the \"Focus on Value\"
principle by ensuring deliverables actually meet customer needs, and
supports stakeholder engagement by addressing concerns proactively
before formal rejection occurs.
erro_alternative1:
Completing the iteration without addressing concerns allows the customer
to formally reject deliverables, damages the relationship, wastes work
that won\'t be accepted, and delays resolution until the next iteration.
erro_alternative3:
Reviewing requirements documentation may confirm compliance with
specifications but doesn\'t address the customer\'s concerns or identify
why there\'s a gap between documented requirements and actual
expectations.
erro_alternative4:
Making quick modifications without understanding specific concerns risks
implementing changes that still don\'t satisfy the customer and may
introduce quality issues through rushed work.

statement:
While working on a public works project for a local government agency,
certain stakeholders are pressuring the project manager to favor their
suggestions over others regarding design elements and resource
allocation. These stakeholders are using their political influence to
push their preferences, creating tension among the stakeholder
community. How should the project manager handle the situation? (Choose two.)
option1: Use the Prioritization method
option2: Use the Salience model
option3:
Meet privately with the most influential stakeholders to negotiate
compromises
option4:
Follow the recommendations of stakeholders with the highest
organizational authority, to secure future favors for the project
option5:
Escalate all competing stakeholder demands to the project sponsor for
resolution
explanation:
The project manager should use structured stakeholder analysis methods
